**Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)** is a non-specific marker for tissue damage (hemolysis, MI, liver disease).
All amino acids in human proteins are in the **L-configuration** (except glycine).
**Glycine** is the only amino acid that is not optically active because it lacks a **chiral center**.
**Non-polar amino acids** (hydrophobic) include valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, and tryptophan.
**Polar uncharged amino acids** include serine, threonine, cysteine, asparagine, and glutamine.
**Acidic amino acids** (negatively charged at pH 7) are **Aspartate** and **Glutamate**.
**Basic amino acids** (positively charged at pH 7) are **Lysine**, **Arginine**, and **Histidine**.
**Essential amino acids** cannot be synthesized by the body and must be obtained from the diet (e.g., PVT TIM HALL).
**Glucogenic amino acids** can be converted into glucose via gluconeogenesis (e.g., alanine, valine).
**Ketogenic amino acids** are converted into acetyl-CoA or acetoacetate; **Leucine** and **Lysine** are exclusively ketogenic.
**Cysteine** contains a **sulfhydryl (-SH) group** and can form **disulfide bonds**.
**Proline** is technically an **imino acid** and causes 'kinks' or 'bends' in protein structures.
The **zwitterion** is the form of an amino acid where the net charge is zero (at its isoelectric point).
The **isoelectric point (pI)** is the pH at which an amino acid has no net electrical charge.
**Tryptophan** is the precursor for **serotonin** and **niacin**.
**Tyrosine** is the precursor for **catecholamines** (dopamine, epinephrine), **thyroid hormones**, and **melanin**.
**Histidine** is the precursor for **histamine**, a mediator of allergic reactions.
**Arginine** is a precursor for **Nitric Oxide (NO)** and is a key member of the urea cycle.
**Phenylketonuria (PKU)** is caused by a deficiency of **phenylalanine hydroxylase**, leading to mental retardation.
**Maple Syrup Urine Disease (MSUD)** is caused by a defect in the metabolism of **branched-chain amino acids** (Val, Leu, Ile).
**Alkaptonuria** is caused by a deficiency of **homogentisic acid oxidase**, resulting in black urine.
**Albinism** is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme **tyrosinase**.
**Selenocysteine** is considered the **21st amino acid**, found in enzymes like glutathione peroxidase.
**GABA** is an inhibitory neurotransmitter derived from the decarboxylation of **glutamate**.
**Buffering action**: Amino acids can act as buffers because their amino and carboxyl groups can accept or donate protons.
**Ninhydrin reaction** is used to detect and quantify amino acids (produces a purple color).
**Glutathione** is a tripeptide (Glu-Cys-Gly) that serves as an important intracellular **antioxidant**.
**Methionine** provides the **methyl group** for various biosynthetic reactions via SAM.
**Creatine** is synthesized from glycine, arginine, and methionine.
**Hydroxyproline and Hydroxylysine** are modified amino acids essential for **collagen** stability.
**Gamma-carboxylation** of glutamate residues (requiring **Vitamin K**) is essential for blood clotting factors.
**Primary structure** is the linear sequence of amino acids linked by **peptide bonds**.
The **peptide bond** is a partial double bond, planar, and rigid, usually in the **trans** configuration.
**Secondary structure** refers to local folding patterns like the **alpha-helix** and **beta-pleated sheet**.
**Alpha-helices** are stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** parallel to the helix axis between the C=O of one amino acid and the N-H four residues away.
**Beta-pleated sheets** are stabilized by hydrogen bonds between adjacent polypeptide strands (can be **parallel** or **anti-parallel**).
**Tertiary structure** is the overall 3D shape of a single polypeptide, stabilized by hydrophobic interactions, ionic bonds, and **disulfide bridges**.
**Quaternary structure** is the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits (e.g., the four subunits of **hemoglobin**).
**Chaperones** (e.g., **Hsp70**) are proteins that assist in the correct folding of other proteins and prevent aggregation.
**Protein denaturation** involves the loss of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure, but the **primary structure remains intact**.
**Alzheimer’s disease** is characterized by the accumulation of **amyloid-beta plaques** and **neurofibrillary tangles** (hyperphosphorylated **tau**).
**Prion diseases** involve the conversion of a normal alpha-helical protein (**PrPc**) into a disease-causing beta-sheet-rich form (**PrPsc**).
**PrPsc** acts as a template to induce further misfolding, leading to **transmissible spongiform encephalopathies**.
**Sickle Cell Anemia** is caused by a single amino acid substitution (Glutamate to **Valine** at position 6 of the beta-globin chain).
**Collagen** is a fibrous protein with a **triple helix** structure, rich in glycine and proline.
**Scurvy** is caused by Vitamin C deficiency, which is a cofactor for **prolyl hydroxylase**, leading to weak collagen.
**Osteogenesis Imperfecta** (brittle bone disease) is often due to mutations in Type I collagen genes.
**Elastin** is a connective tissue protein that allows tissues to stretch and recoil; it is rich in **desmosine** cross-links.
**Alpha-1 Antitrypsin deficiency** leads to uncontrolled **elastase** activity, resulting in emphysema.
**Myoglobin** is a monomeric protein with a **hyperbolic** oxygen dissociation curve.
**Hemoglobin** is a tetrameric protein with a **sigmoidal** oxygen dissociation curve, showing **cooperativity**.
The **Bohr Effect** describes the decrease in hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen in the presence of high CO2 and low pH.
**2,3-BPG** binds to deoxygenated hemoglobin and stabilizes the **T-state**, facilitating oxygen release to tissues.
**Fibrous proteins** (like keratin/collagen) are water-insoluble and serve structural roles.
**Globular proteins** (like enzymes/hemoglobin) are water-soluble and serve functional roles.
**Salting out** is a technique to precipitate proteins using high concentrations of salts like ammonium sulfate.
**Isoelectric focusing** separates proteins based on their **pI** in a pH gradient.
**SDS-PAGE** separates proteins based on their **molecular weight**.
**Heme** is a prosthetic group consisting of a **protoporphyrin IX** ring and a central **ferrous iron (Fe2+)**.
**Carbon monoxide (CO)** has a much higher affinity for hemoglobin than oxygen, causing toxicity.
**Glycation** of hemoglobin (HbA1c) is used to monitor long-term blood glucose levels in diabetics.
**Albumin** is the most abundant plasma protein, synthesized in the **liver**.
The primary function of **albumin** is to maintain the **plasma oncotic pressure**.
**Albumin** also acts as a transport protein for **fatty acids**, **bilirubin**, **calcium**, and many **drugs**.
**Hypoalbuminemia** (low albumin) leads to **edema** because fluid leaks from capillaries into tissues.
**Alpha-1 globulins** include **Alpha-1 antitrypsin** and **Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP)**.
High levels of **AFP** in maternal serum are associated with **neural tube defects**, while low levels suggest **Down syndrome**.
**Alpha-2 globulins** include **Haptoglobin** and **Ceruloplasmin**.
**Haptoglobin** binds to free hemoglobin to prevent its loss in urine and protect the kidneys.
**Ceruloplasmin** is the major **copper-carrying** protein in the blood.
**Wilson’s disease** is characterized by low serum **ceruloplasmin** and copper accumulation in the liver and brain (**Kayser-Fleischer rings**).
**Beta globulins** include **Transferrin**, **C-reactive protein (CRP)**, and **Complement proteins**.
**Transferrin** is the primary protein responsible for transporting **iron** in the plasma.
**C-reactive protein (CRP)** is an **acute-phase reactant** used as a marker for inflammation and infection.
**Gamma globulins** consist of the **immunoglobulins** (antibodies) produced by plasma cells.
**Acute phase proteins** (like CRP, haptoglobin, fibrinogen) increase significantly during inflammation.
**Negative acute phase proteins** (like **albumin** and **transferrin**) decrease during inflammation.
**Fibrinogen** is a beta-globulin essential for **blood coagulation** (converted to fibrin by thrombin).
**Transthyretin** (prealbumin) is a marker of **nutritional status** because of its short half-life.
**Retinol-binding protein (RBP)** transports Vitamin A in the blood.
**Hemopexin** binds free heme to prevent oxidative damage and iron loss.
**Plasma protein electrophoresis** separates proteins into five bands: Albumin, Alpha-1, Alpha-2, Beta, and Gamma.
A **'bridge' between beta and gamma bands** (Beta-gamma bridge) on electrophoresis is characteristic of **liver cirrhosis**.
A **narrow 'M-spike'** in the gamma region is diagnostic for **Multiple Myeloma**.
**Alpha-2 macroglobulin** is a large protease inhibitor that is significantly elevated in **Nephrotic Syndrome**.
**Cryoglobulins** are plasma proteins that precipitate at low temperatures, often seen in Hepatitis C.
**Fetuin-A** is a plasma protein that inhibits ectopic calcification in soft tissues.
**Plasminogen** is the precursor of **plasmin**, the enzyme that dissolves blood clots.
**Sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG)** regulates the availability of free testosterone and estrogen.
**Apolipoproteins** (like ApoB, ApoA1) are the protein components of lipoproteins like LDL and HDL.
**Protein C and Protein S** are vitamin K-dependent plasma proteins that act as natural **anticoagulants**.
**Immunoglobulins (Igs)** are glycoprotein molecules produced by **plasma cells** in response to antigens.
The basic structure of an Ig consists of **two heavy (H) chains** and **two light (L) chains**.
The chains are held together by **disulfide bonds** and non-covalent interactions.
The **Fab region** (Fragment antigen-binding) contains the **variable domains** that recognize the specific antigen.
The **Fc region** (Fragment crystallizable) mediates biological activity like **complement activation** and binding to cell receptors.
**IgG** is the most abundant immunoglobulin in the blood and the only one that can **cross the placenta**.
**IgM** is the first antibody produced during a primary immune response and exists as a **pentamer** in secretion.
**IgA** is the primary antibody found in **secretions** (tears, saliva, colostrum, mucus) and exists as a **dimer** with a **J-chain**.
**IgE** is involved in **allergic reactions** and defense against **parasitic infections**; it binds to **mast cells** and basophils.
**IgD** is found on the surface of B-cells and serves as an **antigen receptor** for B-cell activation.
**B-cells** mature into **plasma cells** upon encountering an antigen and receiving T-cell help.
**Multiple Myeloma** is a malignancy of plasma cells characterized by a monoclonal protein (**M-protein**).
**Bence-Jones proteins** are free **monoclonal light chains** found in the urine of Multiple Myeloma patients.
Classic features of **Multiple Myeloma** include 'punched-out' lytic bone lesions, hypercalcemia, and anemia.
**Cytokines** are small signaling proteins that mediate and regulate immunity and inflammation.
**Interleukin-1 (IL-1)** and **TNF-alpha** are pro-inflammatory cytokines that induce **fever**.
**Interleukin-2 (IL-2)** is essential for the proliferation and activation of **T-cells**.
**Tumor Necrosis Factor (TNF)** can induce **apoptosis** in cancer cells and is a key mediator of septic shock.
**Interferons (IFNs)** are cytokines that primarily provide **anti-viral** protection and activate macrophages.
**Platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)** stimulates cell growth and division, particularly in **wound healing**.
**Platelet-activating factor (PAF)** is a potent phospholipid mediator of inflammation and bronchoconstriction.
**Opsonization** is the process where antibodies (IgG) or complement (C3b) coat a pathogen to enhance **phagocytosis**.
**Complement activation** via the classical pathway is initiated by the binding of **IgM or IgG** to an antigen.
The **J-chain** (Joining chain) is present in the polymeric forms of **IgM and IgA**.
**Isotype switching** is the process where a B-cell changes the class of antibody it produces (e.g., from IgM to IgG) without changing antigen specificity.
**Hypervariable regions** (CDRs) within the variable domains provide the exquisite specificity of antibodies.
**Hybridoma technology** is used to produce **monoclonal antibodies** for clinical and diagnostic use.
**Agammaglobulinemia** (e.g., Bruton's) is a genetic disorder where the patient cannot produce antibodies.
**Waldenström Macroglobulinemia** is a condition involving the overproduction of **IgM**.
**Secretory component** is added to **IgA** as it moves across epithelial cells to protect it from digestion.
The **Limbic System** is the primary anatomical circuit regulating emotions and memory, consisting of the **Hippocampus**, **Amygdala**, and **Cingulate Gyrus**.
The **Hippocampus** is critical for the consolidation of **Short-term Memory** into **Long-term Memory**, specifically declarative memory.
Damage to the **Hippocampus** results in **Anterograde Amnesia**, the inability to form new memories while retaining old ones.
The **Amygdala** is the key structure for processing **Fear** and emotional responses; its ablation leads to **Kluver-Bucy Syndrome** (hyperorality, docility).
The **Hypothalamus** regulates the four 'Fs': Feeding, Fighting, Fleeing, and Mating (Sexual behavior).
**Reward pathways** are primarily mediated by **Dopaminergic** neurons traveling from the **Ventral Tegmental Area (VTA)** to the **Nucleus Accumbens**.
The **Mesolimbic Pathway** is the primary circuit associated with **Addiction** and the feeling of reward/punishment.
**Serotonin (5-HT)** is the neurotransmitter most closely associated with the regulation of **Mood**, sleep, and appetite.
Low levels of **Serotonin** metabolites in cerebrospinal fluid (e.g., **5-HIAA**) are linked to increased impulsivity and **Suicidal Behavior**.
The **Prefrontal Cortex** is responsible for **Executive Function**, impulse control, and social behavior modification.
**Sleep** is regulated by the **Suprachiasmatic Nucleus (SCN)** of the hypothalamus, which acts as the body's master circadian clock.
**Melatonin**, secreted by the **Pineal Gland**, is inhibited by light and stimulated by darkness to induce sleep.
**Non-REM Stage 3 (N3)** sleep is characterized by **Delta Waves** and is the stage where **Sleepwalking** (Somnambulism) occurs.
**REM Sleep** (Rapid Eye Movement) is characterized by **Beta-like waves**, muscle atonia, and vivid dreaming.
The **Reticular Activating System (RAS)** in the brainstem is essential for maintaining **Arousal** and consciousness.
**Acetylcholine** is the primary neurotransmitter involved in **Learning** and memory at the neuromuscular junction and the CNS (depleted in **Alzheimer's**).
**Glutamate** is the brain's major **Excitatory Neurotransmitter**, critical for **Long-term Potentiation (LTP)**.
**GABA** is the brain's major **Inhibitory Neurotransmitter**; its dysfunction is linked to **Anxiety Disorders**.
**Motivation** is driven by the **Drive Reduction Theory**, where physiological needs (thirst/hunger) create an aroused state to be satisfied.
The **Lateral Hypothalamus** acts as the 'hunger center'; its destruction leads to **Anorexia**.
The **Ventromedial Hypothalamus** acts as the 'satiety center'; its destruction leads to **Hyperphagia** (overeating).
**Oxytocin**, often called the 'bonding hormone,' is released by the **Posterior Pituitary** and facilitates social bonding and trust.
**Classical Conditioning** (Pavlovian) involves pairing a **Neutral Stimulus** with an **Unconditioned Stimulus** to elicit a response.
**Operant Conditioning** (Skinnerian) involves learning through **Reinforcement** (positive/negative) and **Punishment**.
**Positive Reinforcement** adds a desirable stimulus to increase a behavior, whereas **Negative Reinforcement** removes an aversive stimulus to increase behavior.
**Punishment** always aims to **Decrease** the frequency of a behavior.
The **Basal Ganglia** are involved in the execution of **Motor Habits** and procedural learning.
**Cortisol** levels naturally peak in the early morning to aid **Arousal** and glucose mobilization.
The **Ventrolateral Preoptic Nucleus (VLPO)** of the hypothalamus is the 'sleep switch' that inhibits the arousal system.
**Prostaglandin D2** is one of the most potent endogenous sleep-promoting substances.
**Testosterone** levels correlate with aggressive behavior and sexual libido in both males and females.
**Estrogen** has neuroprotective effects and influences **Serotonin** receptor sensitivity.
The **Anterior Cingulate Cortex** is involved in the emotional processing of **Pain**.
**Secondary Reinforcers** are learned rewards, such as **Money** or grades, that acquire value through association with primary reinforcers.
**Neuroplasticity** refers to the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life.
**Mirror Neurons** in the premotor cortex fire both when performing an action and observing others, facilitating **Empathy**.
The **Nucleus Basalis of Meynert** is the primary site for **Acetylcholine** production in the brain.
The **Locus Coeruleus** is the primary site for **Norepinephrine** production, regulating the stress response.
The **Raphe Nuclei** are the primary source of **Serotonin** in the brain.
**Synaptic Pruning** is the developmental process where excess neurons and synapses are eliminated to increase circuit efficiency.
**Mental Illness burden** is measured using **DALYs** (Disability-Adjusted Life Years), with depression being a leading cause of global disability.
**Intellectual Disability (ID)** is defined by deficits in **Intellectual Functioning** (IQ < 70) and **Adaptive Functioning** (ADLs).
The severity of **Intellectual Disability** is determined by **Adaptive Functioning** levels rather than IQ scores alone in DSM-5.
**Down Syndrome** (Trisomy 21) is the most common genetic cause of **Intellectual Disability**.
**Fragile X Syndrome** is the most common inherited cause of **Intellectual Disability**, especially in males.
**Schizophrenia** is characterized by 'positive symptoms' like **Hallucinations** (usually auditory) and **Delusions**.
**Negative Symptoms** of Schizophrenia include **Avolition**, **Anhedonia**, and **Al तरह (Alogia)**.
The **Dopamine Hypothesis** of Schizophrenia suggests hyperactivity in the **Mesolimbic Pathway** causes positive symptoms.
**Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)** requires at least two weeks of low mood or **Anhedonia** (loss of interest).
**Bipolar I Disorder** is defined by the occurrence of at least one **Manic Episode**, which may or may not be preceded by depression.
**Bipolar II Disorder** requires at least one **Hypomanic Episode** and one Major Depressive episode.
**Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)** is characterized by excessive worry about various events for at least **6 Months**.
**Panic Disorder** involves recurrent, unexpected **Panic Attacks** followed by persistent concern about future attacks.
**Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)** involves **Obsessions** (intrusive thoughts) and **Compulsions** (repetitive behaviors to reduce anxiety).
**Personality Disorders** are enduring patterns of behavior that deviate from cultural expectations, divided into **Clusters A, B, and C**.
**Cluster A** (Odd/Eccentric) includes **Paranoid**, **Schizoid**, and **Schizotypal** Personality Disorders.
**Schizoid Personality Disorder** is characterized by a preference for **Social Isolation** and a lack of interest in relationships.
**Schizotypal Personality Disorder** involves **Magical Thinking** and eccentric behavior, but not full psychosis.
**Cluster B** (Dramatic/Erratic) includes **Antisocial**, **Borderline**, **Histrionic**, and **Narcissistic** types.
**Antisocial Personality Disorder** requires evidence of **Conduct Disorder** before age 15 and a disregard for the rights of others.
**Borderline Personality Disorder** is characterized by instability in relationships, self-image, and **Splitting** (idealization vs. devaluation).
**Histrionic Personality Disorder** is characterized by **Attention-seeking** behavior and excessive emotionality.
**Cluster C** (Anxious/Fearful) includes **Avoidant**, **Dependent**, and **Obsessive-Compulsive** Personality Disorders.
**Avoidant Personality Disorder** involves hypersensitivity to rejection and feelings of inadequacy, despite a desire for relationships.
**Obsessive-Compulsive Personality Disorder (OCPD)** is **Ego-syntonic** (the person likes their orderliness), unlike OCD which is Ego-dystonic.
**Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)** diagnosis requires symptoms lasting more than **1 Month** following a traumatic event.
**Somatization** is the expression of psychological distress through **Physical Symptoms**.
**Anorexia Nervosa** is characterized by a restricted food intake leading to significantly low body weight and **Body Dysmorphia**.
**Bulimia Nervosa** involves cycles of **Binge Eating** followed by compensatory behaviors like purging or excessive exercise.
**Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)** involves persistent patterns of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity before age 12.
**Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)** is marked by deficits in **Social Communication** and restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior.
**Dementia** (Major Neurocognitive Disorder) involves a significant decline in one or more cognitive domains that interferes with independence.
**Alzheimer's Disease** is the most common cause of dementia, pathologically marked by **Amyloid Plaques** and **Tau Tangles**.
**Vascular Dementia** results from multiple infarcts and typically follows a **Stepwise Decline** in function.
**Delirium** is an acute, fluctuating state of confusion and altered consciousness, often reversible.
**Alcohol Use Disorder** can lead to **Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome** due to **Thiamine (B1)** deficiency.
**Agoraphobia** is the fear of being in situations where escape might be difficult or help unavailable in the event of a panic attack.
**Social Anxiety Disorder** is an intense fear of being scrutinized or negatively evaluated by others in social situations.
**Specific Phobias** are treated primarily with **Exposure Therapy** (systematic desensitization).
**Stigma** associated with mental illness significantly reduces **Health-seeking Behavior** and treatment adherence.
The **Biopsychosocial Model** posits that health and illness are determined by an interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors.
**Type A Personality** (characterized by hostility, urgency, and competitiveness) is a risk factor for **Coronary Heart Disease (CHD)**.
**Hostility** is the specific component of Type A behavior most strongly correlated with **Myocardial Infarction**.
**Depression** is an independent risk factor for poor prognosis following a **Heart Attack**.
Psychosocial stress can lead to **Hypertension** via chronic activation of the **Sympathetic Nervous System**.
**Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)** is a classic functional GIT disorder where stress exacerbates abdominal pain and altered bowel habits.
The **Gut-Brain Axis** involves bidirectional communication between the enteric nervous system and the CNS, influenced by the **Microbiome**.
Stress-induced **Hypercortisolemia** can lead to the development of **Peptic Ulcers** by reducing gastric mucosal protection.
In **Respiratory Diseases**, emotional stress can trigger **Bronchoconstriction** in patients with asthma.
**Hyperventilation Syndrome** is a psychological response to anxiety that causes **Respiratory Alkalosis** and peripheral paresthesia.
Chronic stress is linked to **Insulin Resistance**, contributing to the onset and poor control of **Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus**.
Psychosocial factors in **Cancer** patients, such as low social support, are associated with faster disease progression and higher mortality.
The term **Psychoneuroimmunology** refers to the study of how psychological states affect the immune system.
Stress increases the secretion of **Pro-inflammatory Cytokines** (e.g., IL-6), which can worsen systemic inflammation.
**Endocrine Disorders** like Graves' disease can be precipitated or worsened by acute emotional trauma.
**Cushing's Syndrome** (excess cortisol) often presents with psychological symptoms such as **Depression** or Psychosis.
In **Chronic Renal Disease**, the psychological burden of dialysis leads to high rates of **Treatment Non-adherence**.
**Phantom Limb Pain** after amputation is a psychological and neurological phenomenon influenced by pre-operative pain levels.
**Erectile Dysfunction** often has a significant **Psychogenic** component, even when organic factors are present.
**Stress-induced Cardiomyopathy** (Takotsubo) is a 'broken heart syndrome' triggered by severe emotional distress.
Patients with **Chronic Pain** often develop **Learned Helplessness**, a psychological state where they feel they have no control over their condition.
**Placebo Effect** demonstrates the power of psychological expectation in producing physiological healing.
**Nocebo Effect** occurs when negative expectations by the patient lead to a worsening of symptoms.
Social isolation is as significant a risk factor for **Mortality** as smoking or obesity.
**Caregiver Strain** is a psychosocial phenomenon where the person looking after a chronically ill patient suffers from burnout and depression.
In **Rheumatoid Arthritis**, stress flares are associated with increased **Prostaglandin** release.
**Psoriasis** and **Eczema** are dermatological conditions frequently exacerbated by psychological stress.
**Type D Personality** (Distressed) is characterized by negative affectivity and social inhibition, linked to poor CVS outcomes.
**Alexithymia**, the inability to identify and describe emotions, is common in patients with **Somatosomatic Disorders**.
**Illness Anxiety Disorder** (formerly Hypochondriasis) involves preoccupation with having a serious undiagnosed illness despite medical reassurance.
**Conversion Disorder** involves unexplained sensory or motor deficits (e.g., blindness, paralysis) following a psychological stressor.
**Factitious Disorder** (Munchausen) involves intentional falsification of symptoms to assume the **Sick Role**.
**Malingering** is the intentional faking of illness for **Secondary Gain** (e.g., money, avoiding work).
**Lifestyle Medicine** focuses on behavioral changes (diet, exercise) to treat and reverse chronic diseases.
**Brief Intervention** is a psychological technique used in primary care to address risky alcohol or substance use.
**Cognitive Appraisal** is the psychological process of evaluating whether a stressor is a threat or a challenge.
**Hardiness** is a personality trait (commitment, control, challenge) that protects individuals from the ill effects of stress.
**Social Support** acts as a 'buffer' against the physiological effects of chronic disease.
**Doctor-Patient Communication** quality is a major determinant of patient psychological adjustment to cancer diagnosis.
**Health Literacy** is the degree to which individuals can obtain and understand basic health information to make decisions.
**Treatment Compliance** (Adherence) is the extent to which a patient's behavior coincides with medical advice.
The **Health Belief Model (HBM)** predicts health behaviors based on perceived susceptibility, severity, benefits, and barriers.
**Perceived Barriers** is often the single most powerful predictor of health behavior change in the HBM.
**Self-Efficacy**, added later to the HBM, is the confidence in one's ability to take action.
**Non-compliance** is highest in chronic, asymptomatic diseases like **Hypertension** and **Dyslipidemia**.
**Complexity of Regimen** (e.g., multiple doses per day) is a major social factor leading to poor drug adherence.
**Health Literacy** is a significant barrier; patients who cannot read labels are less likely to comply with treatments.
The **Prescriber-Patient Relationship** is the foundation of adherence; trust increases the likelihood of following advice.
**Social Support** (family/friends) significantly improves adherence in elderly patients with polypharmacy.
**Drug Resistance** can be a behavioral issue, often caused by patients stopping **Antibiotics** prematurely when symptoms improve.
**Polypharmacy** (taking 5+ medications) increases the risk of drug-drug interactions and non-compliance.
**Side Effects** (e.g., sexual dysfunction from SSRIs) are a primary reason for patients discontinuing psychiatric medications.
**Social Stigma** regarding mental illness often prevents patients from filling prescriptions for **Antipsychotics**.
**Cost of Medication** is a critical socioeconomic factor in drug non-adherence in low-income populations.
**Motivational Interviewing** is a clinical technique used to enhance a patient's intrinsic motivation for treatment adherence.
**Internal Locus of Control** refers to the belief that one's own actions determine health outcomes, improving adherence.
**External Locus of Control** refers to the belief that fate or doctors control health, which can sometimes decrease self-care.
**Treatment Inertia** occurs when providers fail to escalate treatment despite not reaching clinical goals.
**White Coat Hypertension** is a behavioral response to the clinical setting that leads to false high blood pressure readings.
**Psychological Reactance** is when a patient resists a treatment because they feel their freedom of choice is being threatened.
**Cultural Beliefs** about 'natural' versus 'synthetic' medicine significantly influence drug acceptance.
**Forgetfulness** is the most commonly cited reason for unintentional non-adherence.
**Visual Aids** and pill organizers are behavioral interventions that improve compliance in cognitive impairment.
**Directly Observed Therapy (DOT)** is a strategy used in **Tuberculosis** treatment to ensure 100% adherence.
The **Transtheoretical Model** (Stages of Change) includes Precontemplation, Contemplation, Preparation, Action, and Maintenance.
In the **Precontemplation** stage, the patient has no intention of changing behavior in the next 6 months.
**Relapse** is considered a normal part of the behavioral change cycle, not a failure of treatment.
**Secondary Non-adherence** occurs when a patient fills a prescription but does not take the medication as directed.
**Primary Non-adherence** occurs when a patient never fills the initial prescription given by the doctor.
**Shared Decision Making** increases the 'buy-in' from the patient, leading to better pharmacological outcomes.
**Fear Appeals** in health education (e.g., showing lung cancer images) can backfire if the patient feels no self-efficacy.
**Bioavailability** can be affected by behavioral factors like taking medication with/without food or with alcohol.
**Doctor Shopping** is a behavior often seen in prescription drug misuse (e.g., **Benzodiazepines** or Opioids).
**Withdrawal Symptoms** are a powerful behavioral motivator for continued drug use in physical dependence.
**Tachyphylaxis** is a rapid decrease in response to a drug, which can lead to patients increasing doses dangerously.
**Cues to Action** in the Health Belief Model are triggers (like a doctor's call or a symptom) that prompt behavior.
**Socioeconomic Status (SES)** is a distal factor that influences every aspect of pharmacological treatment access.
**Telemedicine** has been shown to improve adherence by reducing the 'barrier' of travel and time.
**Health Psychology** interventions can reduce the need for pharmacological doses in conditions like **Insomnia** (CBT-I).
**Empowerment** of the patient leads to 'Concordance,' where doctor and patient agree on the therapeutic plan.
**Palliative Care** focuses on improving the quality of life for patients and families facing life-threatening illness, regardless of the stage.
**Rehabilitation** in dialysis patients aims to maintain muscle mass and manage the psychological burden of **Uremia**.
**Depression** occurs in up to 30% of patients on chronic **Dialysis** and is linked to higher mortality.
**Diabetes Mellitus** requires lifelong behavioral management; 'Diabetes Distress' describes the emotional burden of the disease.
**Multi-infarct Dementia** (Vascular Dementia) requires palliative focus on safety, routine, and caregiver support.
**Chronic Renal Disease (CKD)** palliative care involves managing symptoms like **Pruritus**, fatigue, and fluid overload.
**Limb Amputation** involves a grieving process similar to the death of a loved one (Kübler-Ross stages).
**Phantom Limb Sensation** is the feeling that the limb is still there, while **Phantom Limb Pain** is a painful sensation.
**Mirror Box Therapy** is a behavioral/neurological intervention used to treat phantom limb pain.
**Advance Directives** (Living Wills) are crucial in palliative care to ensure the patient's end-of-life wishes are respected.
**Hospice Care** is a specific type of palliative care for patients with a prognosis of **6 Months** or less to live.
**Total Pain** is a concept in palliative care that includes physical, psychological, social, and spiritual dimensions.
**Breakthrough Pain** is a sudden flare of pain that occurs despite continuous background analgesia.
**Laxatives** should almost always be prescribed alongside **Opioids** in palliative care to prevent constipation.
**Mouth Care** is essential in palliative patients to prevent **Xerostomia** (dry mouth) and candidiasis.
**Dyspnea** in end-stage heart or lung disease is often managed with low-dose **Morphine** to reduce the sensation of breathlessness.
**Cachexia** (wasting) in cancer and end-stage renal disease is a complex metabolic syndrome, not just malnutrition.
**Anxiety** is common in palliative care and can be managed with **Benzodiazepines** or supportive psychotherapy.
**The 'Death Rattle'** is caused by the accumulation of secretions in the pharynx and is managed with **Antimuscarinics** like Hyoscine.
**Bereavement Support** for the family is a core component of the palliative care model.
**Respite Care** provides temporary relief for primary caregivers to prevent burnout.
**Autonomy** is the ethical principle most emphasized when discussing palliative options with a patient.
**Non-maleficence** (do no harm) in palliative care means avoiding futile treatments that prolong suffering without benefit.
**Beneficence** involves acting in the patient's best interest to provide comfort and dignity.
**Spiritual Distress** is a common component of palliative care requiring a multidisciplinary team (e.g., chaplains).
**Activities of Daily Living (ADLs)** such as bathing and dressing are key targets for rehabilitation in the disabled.
**Instrumental ADLs (IADLs)** like managing finances or cooking are higher-level functions assessed in dementia.
**Occupational Therapy** plays a vital role in adapting the environment for patients with limb amputations.
**Physiotherapy** is crucial for preventing contractures in bedbound palliative patients.
**The 'Bad News' protocol (SPIKES)**: Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy.
**Double Effect** is the principle where a treatment (like morphine) is given to relieve pain, even if it might hasten death as a side effect.
**Agitated Delirium** at the end of life is often treated with **Haloperidol**.
**Skin Integrity** (preventing pressure ulcers) is a major nursing goal in palliative rehabilitation.
**Nutritional Support** in palliative care should focus on pleasure rather than caloric goals in the final stages.
**Social Isolation** in the elderly with disability increases the risk of **Cognitive Decline**.
**Transition of Care** from curative to palliative requires clear communication to avoid feelings of 'abandonment.'
**End-stage Congestive Heart Failure (CHF)** palliative care focuses on volume status and reducing 'air hunger.'
**Cognitive Stimulation Therapy** is a behavioral rehab approach for patients with mild-to-moderate dementia.
**Grief** is a normal response, while **Persistent Complex Bereavement Disorder** is a pathological extension of grief.
**Dignity Therapy** is a brief psychotherapy designed to help terminally ill patients find meaning.
**Stress** is a state of threatened homeostasis, triggered by physical or psychological 'stressors.'
The **Sympathetic-Adrenal-Medullary (SAM) Axis** provides the 'Fight-or-Flight' response via **Epinephrine**.
The **Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal (HPA) Axis** is the slower, more sustained stress response pathway.
**CRH (Corticotropin-Releasing Hormone)** is released by the **Hypothalamus** in response to stress.
**ACTH (Adrenocorticotropic Hormone)** is released by the **Anterior Pituitary** to stimulate the adrenal cortex.
**Cortisol** is the primary 'stress hormone' that increases blood glucose and suppresses non-essential functions.
**Hans Selye’s General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS)** consists of three stages: **Alarm**, **Resistance**, and **Exhaustion**.
In the **Exhaustion Stage** of GAS, the body’s resources are depleted, leading to illness or death.
**Allostatic Load** is the 'wear and tear' on the body caused by chronic stress and over-activation of stress systems.
Chronic stress causes **Atrophy of the Hippocampus**, leading to memory deficits.
Stress causes **Hypertrophy of the Amygdala**, leading to increased anxiety and emotional reactivity.
**Epinephrine** and **Norepinephrine** cause increased heart rate, bronchodilation, and shunting of blood to muscles.
**Cortisol** inhibits the production of **Interleukin-1 (IL-1)** and **Tumor Necrosis Factor (TNF)**, suppressing immunity.
**Psychological Manifestations** of stress include irritability, 'brain fog,' and decreased concentration.
**Behavioral Manifestations** of stress include 'emotional eating,' substance abuse, and social withdrawal.
**Multiple Sclerosis (MS)** relapses are significantly associated with major life stressors and high cortisol levels.
Stress triggers **Autoimmune Diseases** by altering the balance of **Th1 and Th2** immune responses.
**Rheumatoid Arthritis** flares are often preceded by periods of high emotional stress.
Chronic stress promotes **Atherosclerosis** by increasing systemic inflammation and blood pressure.
**Telomere Shortening** is accelerated by chronic stress, leading to premature cellular aging.
**Type 1 Diabetes** can be influenced by stress, which may trigger the autoimmune destruction of beta cells in susceptible individuals.
**Stress-induced Immunosuppression** increases susceptibility to the common cold and viral infections.
**Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)** is associated with a hyper-responsive HPA axis and low baseline cortisol in some studies.
**Coping** is the process of managing demands that are appraised as taxing or exceeding resources.
**Problem-Focused Coping** aims to change the stressor itself (best for controllable stressors).
**Emotion-Focused Coping** aims to regulate the emotional response to the stressor (best for uncontrollable stressors).
**Rumination** is a maladaptive coping style involving repetitive thinking about the causes and consequences of stress.
**Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)** is an evidence-based behavioral intervention to lower cortisol.
The **Parasympathetic Nervous System** (via the Vagus nerve) acts as the 'brake' to the stress response.
**Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is a measure of the balance between sympathetic and parasympathetic activity; low HRV indicates high stress.
Stress exacerbates **Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)** by triggering cytokine storms.
The **Prefrontal Cortex** normally inhibits the stress response; chronic stress weakens this 'top-down' control.
**Oxidative Stress** is a biochemical consequence of chronic physiological stress, damaging DNA and proteins.
**Workplace Burnout** is a state of vital exhaustion resulting from chronic occupational stress.
**Caregiver Stress** is associated with delayed **Wound Healing** due to high cortisol levels.
**Sleep Deprivation** is both a stressor and a result of stress, creating a vicious cycle of HPA axis activation.
**Secondary Traumatic Stress** (Compassion Fatigue) affects healthcare workers exposed to others' trauma.
**The 'Tending and Befriending' Response** is a stress response characterized by social grooming, more common in females (linked to Oxytocin).
**Psychosomatic** refers to physical diseases that are caused or aggravated by mental factors like stress.
**Stress Management** training includes techniques like Biofeedback, Progressive Muscle Relaxation, and CBT.
The **Biomedical Concept** of health traditionally views health as the **absence of disease**, often referred to as the 'germ theory' era.
The **Ecological Concept** views health as a **dynamic equilibrium** between man and his environment.
In the **Psychosocial Concept**, health is influenced by **social, psychological, cultural, and economic** factors, not just biological ones.
The **Holistic Concept** recognizes the strength of social, economic, political, and environmental influences, emphasizing the **well-being of the whole person**.
The **WHO definition (1948)** states health is a state of **complete physical, mental, and social well-being** and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.
A critical limitation of the WHO definition is that it describes health as a **static state** rather than a dynamic process.
The **New Philosophy of Health** views health as a **fundamental human right** and a prerequisite for social and economic development.
Health is now considered an **inter-sectoral** issue, requiring coordination between agriculture, education, and housing, not just the health department.
The **Responsibility for Health** starts at the **Individual level**, focusing on self-care and healthy lifestyle choices.
**Community Responsibility** involves participation in local health programs and maintaining a sanitary environment.
**State Responsibility** entails providing adequate health services, clean water, and nutritional support through government policy.
**International Responsibility** is managed by bodies like the **WHO** and **UNICEF** to manage global pandemics and health equity.
The concept of **Positive Health** implies that an individual is functioning at the **optimal level** of their potential.
Health is considered a **productive tool**, essential for the economic growth of a nation.
The **Socialist Concept** of health suggests that health is a **state property** and the government is solely responsible for it.
**Self-Care** is the first line of health action and includes activities like hygiene, nutrition, and environmental sanitation.
The **Ottawa Charter** shifted the focus towards **Health Promotion**, enabling people to increase control over their health.
The **Medical Model** focuses on treating the individual patient, whereas the **Public Health Model** focuses on the population.
Health is a **dynamic process**, meaning it changes along a continuum from 'optimal health' to 'death'.
The **Internal Environment** of health refers to the harmonious functioning of all **body systems** and organs.
The **External Environment** refers to everything outside the human host, categorized into **physical, biological, and social** factors.
The 'Philosophy of Health' considers health as an **investment**, not just a consumption item.
Health as a **Macro-level Goal** is measured through national indicators like **Life Expectancy**.
The transition from the 'Medical Model' to the 'Social Model' emphasizes **preventive medicine** over curative medicine.
**Health Awareness** is the primary step in individual responsibility, leading to behavioral change.
The **Global Health Philosophy** emphasizes that 'Disease knows no boundaries', requiring international surveillance.
**Individual Responsibility** includes compliance with medical treatment and avoiding **risk factors** like smoking.
**Right to Health** is enshrined in the constitution of many countries as a derivative of the **Right to Life**.
The **Changing Concept of Health** moves away from 'death-control' to 'health-promotion'.
**Quality of Life** is a subjective measure often used to assess the success of health interventions.
**Community participation** is the 'heart' of Primary Health Care (PHC).
The **holistic model** of health is often symbolized by the 'Multi-axial' approach to diagnosis.
**Health Literacy** is a key determinant in how individuals exercise their responsibility for health.
The **state's role** in health is often evaluated by the percentage of **GDP** allocated to the health sector.
**International health regulations** are binding instruments of international law to prevent the spread of disease.
Health is no longer seen as the 'end point' but as a **resource for everyday life**.
The concept of **Well-being** incorporates both 'standard of living' and 'quality of life'.
**Subjective Well-being** is measured by self-reported happiness or life satisfaction.
**Objective Well-being** is measured by external standards like income and housing conditions.
The **philosophy of social justice** in health ensures that health resources are distributed according to **need**.

nuggets.lo_heading: F-CM-002. Positive Health Dimensions, Health Determinants
content: **Dimensions of Health** include Physical, Mental, Social, Spiritual, Emotional, and Vocational aspects.
**Physical Dimension** is characterized by 'perfect functioning' of the body, where every organ is working at **optimum capacity**.
Signs of physical health include a **clean skin**, bright eyes, lustrous hair, and a coordinated body movement.
**Mental Dimension** is defined as a state of balance between the individual and the surrounding world, characterized by **self-actualization**.
A mentally healthy person is free from **internal conflicts** and has a good sense of self-esteem.
**Social Dimension** involves the quantity and quality of an individual’s **interpersonal ties** and community involvement.
**Spiritual Dimension** refers to that part of the individual which reaches out and strives for **meaning and purpose** in life.
**Determinants of Health** are the factors that influence the health status of individuals or populations.
**Biological Determinants** include **genetics**, chromosomal anomalies, and the aging process.
**Behavioral Determinants** (Lifestyle) include habits like smoking, alcohol consumption, and **physical inactivity**.
**Environmental Determinants** are divided into 'Macro-environment' (climate, air) and 'Micro-environment' (**domestic living conditions**).
**Socio-economic Determinants** include income, education, occupation, and **social status**.
**Education**, especially female literacy, is a major determinant of health outcomes in developing countries.
**Occupation** affects health through exposure to **workplace hazards** and physical stress.
**Health Services** as a determinant refers to the availability and accessibility of **immunization** and primary care.
**Positive Health** is a state where an individual enjoys perfect functioning of body and mind; it is the 'ceiling' of the health spectrum.
**Wellbeing** is a multifaceted concept consisting of **Standard of Living** and **Quality of Life**.
**Standard of Living** is an objective measure comprising income, housing, and nutrition (often measured by **per capita GNP**).
**Quality of Life** is subjective and reflects the satisfaction or dissatisfaction with life conditions.
The **Physical Quality of Life Index (PQLI)** is a composite index of three indicators: **Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)**, **Life Expectancy at Age 1**, and **Literacy**.
PQLI scale ranges from **0 to 100**; it does not include per capita income.
The **Human Development Index (HDI)** is the 'gold standard' for measuring development, focusing on **longevity, knowledge, and income**.
HDI components include **Life Expectancy at Birth**, **Mean years of schooling**, **Expected years of schooling**, and **GNI per capita**.
The value of HDI ranges from **0 to 1**; a value above **0.8** is considered 'Very High Human Development'.
**GNI per capita (PPP $)** is used in HDI to reflect the 'Standard of Living' dimension.
**Life Expectancy at Birth** in HDI reflects the 'Longevity' or health dimension.
**Mean and Expected years of schooling** in HDI reflect the 'Education' dimension.
A major difference: **PQLI** uses Life Expectancy at Age 1, while **HDI** uses Life Expectancy at Birth.
**Social Determinants of Health (SDOH)** are the 'conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age'.
**Gender** is a social determinant, as women often face higher health risks due to **biological and social factors**.
**Internal Determinants** are those within the body (e.g., **metabolic rate**, genetic predisposition).
**External Determinants** are factors outside the body (e.g., **pathogens**, pollutants).
**Nutrition** is a fundamental determinant; both **undernutrition** and **overnutrition** lead to health problems.
**Political systems** determine health through the allocation of resources and **health policies**.
The **Ecological Balance** determinant focuses on the relationship between man and other **biological species**.
**Urbanization** is a determinant that can lead to overcrowding and the spread of **infectious diseases**.
**Life Expectancy** is a sensitive indicator of the overall health determinant profile of a country.
**Positive Health** requires the 'integration' of all dimensions, not just the absence of illness.
**Vocational Dimension** involves finding satisfaction in one’s work, which contributes to **mental health**.
The **Philosophical Dimension** involves an individual’s outlook on life and their **resilience** to stress.
A **Health Indicator** is a variable that can be measured directly to reflect the health status of a community.
**Characteristics of a good indicator** include: Valid, Reliable, Sensitive, Specific, Feasible, and Relevant.
**Validity** means the indicator measures what it is intended to measure.
**Reliability** means the indicator yields the same results when measured by different observers.
**Sensitivity** refers to the indicator's ability to detect small changes in the health status.
**Specificity** means the indicator reflects changes only in the specific condition under study.
**Mortality Indicators** measure the occurrence of death in a population.
**Crude Death Rate (CDR)** is the number of deaths per 1,000 population per year.
**Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)** is the number of deaths of infants under 1 year of age per 1,000 live births.
**IMR** is considered one of the most **sensitive indicators** of a community's health status and socio-economic level.
**Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR)** is the number of maternal deaths per 100,000 live births.
**Life Expectancy** is the average number of years a person is expected to live if current mortality trends continue.
**Morbidity Indicators** measure the frequency of illness, including **Incidence** and **Prevalence**.
**Incidence** is the number of **new cases** occurring in a defined population during a specific period.
**Prevalence** is the total number of **existing cases** (old + new) in a population at a given point in time.
**Disability Indicators** measure the impact of disease on functioning; examples include **DALY** and **HALE**.
**DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)** equals the sum of **Years of Life Lost (YLL)** and **Years Lived with Disability (YLD)**.
One DALY represents the loss of **one year of 'healthy' life**.
**HALE (Health-Adjusted Life Expectancy)** is the average number of years a person can expect to live in **full health**.
**Sullivan’s Index** (Disability-free life expectancy) is calculated by subtracting the duration of bed disability from life expectancy.
**Nutritional Status Indicators** include **anthropometric measurements** like weight-for-age and mid-upper arm circumference.
**Health Care Delivery Indicators** include the **Doctor-Population Ratio** and **Nurse-Patient Ratio**.
**Utilization Rates** measure how well health services are used, e.g., **Bed Occupancy Rate** or **Immunization Coverage**.
**Social and Mental Health Indicators** include rates of suicide, homicide, and **alcohol/drug abuse**.
**Environmental Indicators** measure access to **safe water** and sanitation facilities.
**Socio-economic Indicators** include GNI per capita, literacy rate, and **dependency ratio**.
**Health Policy Indicators** include the percentage of **GNP** spent on health and the percentage of resources for primary care.
**Case Fatality Rate (CFR)** measures the **killing power** of a disease and is expressed as a percentage.
**Proportional Mortality Rate** measures the proportion of deaths due to a specific cause relative to all deaths.
**Under-5 Mortality Rate** is the probability of dying between birth and exactly 5 years of age per 1,000 live births.
**Neonatal Mortality Rate** includes deaths within the first **28 days** of life.
**Post-neonatal Mortality Rate** includes deaths from 28 days to **under 1 year**.
**Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR)** is used to compare mortality between different populations by adjusting for **age and sex**.
**Global Burden of Disease (GBD)** is a study that uses **DALYs** to compare the impact of different diseases globally.
Indicators are used to **set priorities**, evaluate health programs, and compare health status among countries.
The **Dependency Ratio** is the ratio of persons in the 'dependent' ages (under 15 and over 64) to those in the 'economically productive' ages.
**Health for All (HFA)** indicators were developed by WHO to monitor progress towards global health goals.
Comparing indicators among countries reveals **health inequities** and the 'Epidemiological Transition'.
**High-income countries** usually show high HALE and low IMR/MMR.
**Low-income countries** often have high communicable disease burdens and high **Under-5 mortality**.
**Disease Causation** has evolved from supernatural theories to the modern **multifactorial causation** model.
The **Germ Theory of Disease**, popularized by **Louis Pasteur** and **Robert Koch**, states that a specific microbe causes a specific disease.
**Henle-Koch's Postulates** are used to establish a causal relationship between a microbe and a disease.
The **Epidemiological Triad** consists of three components: **Agent, Host, and Environment**.
**Agent Factors** include biological (bacteria, virus), chemical (toxins), physical (heat, radiation), and nutrient (vitamins) agents.
**Host Factors** include age, sex, **genetic profile**, immunity, and behavior (lifestyle).
**Environmental Factors** include physical (water, air), biological (vectors, reservoirs), and social (overcrowding) conditions.
The **Web of Causation** (suggested by MacMahon and Pugh) is used for **chronic diseases** where multiple factors interact.
In the **Web of Causation**, there is no single 'cause'; instead, factors like diet, exercise, and genetics are **interlinked**.
**Multifactorial Causation** implies that disease is the result of multiple causes acting together (e.g., **Coronary Heart Disease**).
The **Natural History of Disease** is the course of a disease from its beginning to its final clinical outcome without intervention.
The two phases of Natural History are **Pre-pathogenesis** and **Pathogenesis**.
**Pre-pathogenesis Phase** occurs in the environment before the man is involved; the agent and host are yet to interact.
**Pathogenesis Phase** begins with the **entry of the agent** into the human host.
The **Incubation Period** is the time interval between the entry of the pathogen and the appearance of the first clinical sign.
**Subclinical Infection** occurs when the host has the pathogen but shows no overt signs or symptoms.
The **Gradient of Infection** (Spectrum of Disease) refers to the sequence of manifestations from subclinical to fatal cases.
The **Iceberg Phenomenon** of disease describes how only a small portion of a disease in a community is visible (diagnosed).
In the Iceberg Phenomenon, the **Tip** represents clinical cases, while the **Submerged Portion** represents subclinical, undiagnosed, and carrier states.
Diseases like **Hypertension, Diabetes, and Malnutrition** show a prominent iceberg phenomenon.
**Risk Factors** are characteristics or exposures that increase the likelihood of developing a disease (e.g., **smoking** for lung cancer).
A **Risk Group** is a specific part of the population that is more likely to develop a disease (e.g., **pregnant women** for anemia).
The **Biological Onset** of disease is the moment the pathological process starts, which often precedes clinical symptoms.
**Carrier State** occurs when an individual harbors a pathogen and can transmit it, but shows no symptoms (e.g., **Typhoid Mary**).
**Vector** is an animate intermediary (like a mosquito) that carries the agent from a reservoir to a host.
**Vehicle** is an inanimate object (like water or food) that transmits an infectious agent.
**Zoonosis** is an infectious disease that is transmissible from animals to humans (e.g., **Rabies**).
**Epizootic** is an outbreak of disease in an animal population (e.g., **Anthrax** in cattle).
**Enzootic** is a disease that is constantly present in an animal population in a specific area.
**Endemic** refers to the constant presence of a disease within a geographic area.
**Epidemic** is the occurrence of cases of an illness in a community in excess of **normal expectancy**.
**Pandemic** is an epidemic that has spread over several countries or continents, affecting a large number of people.
**Sporadic** cases occur irregularly, haphazardly, and infrequently.
**Direct Transmission** includes skin-to-skin contact, droplet spread, and contact with soil.
**Indirect Transmission** includes air-borne, vehicle-borne, and vector-borne routes.
**Vertical Transmission** is the passage of a pathogen from mother to fetus via the **placenta** or milk.
**Horizontal Transmission** is the spread of an agent among members of the same generation.
**Infectivity** is the ability of an agent to enter and multiply in a host.
**Virulence** is the degree of **pathogenicity** or the severity of the disease produced.
**Antigenicity** is the ability of the agent to induce an immune response in the host.
**Prevention** is the process of taking steps to stop a disease from occurring or to minimize its impact.
**Primordial Prevention** focuses on preventing the **emergence of risk factors** in a population (e.g., encouraging children not to start smoking).
**Primary Prevention** involves action taken **before the onset** of disease, aiming to remove the possibility that a disease will ever occur.
Modes of intervention for Primary Prevention include **Health Promotion** and **Specific Protection**.
**Health Promotion** interventions include health education, environmental modifications, and nutritional interventions.
**Specific Protection** includes **immunization**, use of specific nutrients (like Iodine), and protection against occupational hazards.
**Secondary Prevention** involves action which **halts the progress** of a disease at its incipient stage and prevents complications.
The 'tools' of secondary prevention are **Early Diagnosis** and **Prompt Treatment** (e.g., screening for breast cancer).
Secondary Prevention is essentially 'curative' but serves as 'preventive' for the rest of the community by shortening the period of communicability.
**Tertiary Prevention** consists of measures taken when the disease has advanced beyond the early stages.
The modes of intervention for Tertiary Prevention are **Disability Limitation** and **Rehabilitation**.
**Disability Limitation** aims to prevent the transition from impairment to handicap (e.g., proper positioning of limbs in **Polio**).
**Rehabilitation** is the combined use of medical, social, and vocational measures for training the individual to the highest level of functional ability.
The **Five Levels of Intervention** (Leavell and Clark) are: 1. Health Promotion, 2. Specific Protection, 3. Early Diagnosis, 4. Disability Limitation, 5. Rehabilitation.
**Disease Control** refers to reducing the incidence, prevalence, morbidity, or mortality of a disease to a locally acceptable level.
**Disease Elimination** means the interruption of transmission of a disease from a **large geographic area** (e.g., Measles elimination).
**Disease Eradication** is the **permanent reduction to zero** of the worldwide incidence of an infection (e.g., **Smallpox**).
**Surveillance** is the continuous scrutiny of all aspects of occurrence and spread of a disease.
The **Surveillance Cycle** includes data collection, analysis, interpretation, and **dissemination** of results for action.
**Passive Surveillance** is when health authorities rely on health workers or hospitals to report cases (most common type).
**Active Surveillance** is when health staff visit communities or households to **actively look for cases** (e.g., during a polio campaign).
**Sentinel Surveillance** involves monitoring a **key group** or specific site to identify trends in the larger population (used for **HIV/AIDS**).
**Monitoring** is the performance and analysis of routine measurements aimed at detecting changes in the environment or health status.
The main difference between surveillance and monitoring is that surveillance is **continuous**, while monitoring may be periodic.
**Quarantine** is the limitation of movement of **healthy persons** who have been exposed to a communicable disease.
**Isolation** is the separation of **infected persons** from others for the period of communicability.
**Mass Screening** is the application of tests to large populations to identify unrecognized diseases.
**High-risk Strategy** in prevention targets only those at greatest risk, while **Population Strategy** targets the entire population.
**Prophylaxis** is a specific measure (like giving **Chloroquine**) to prevent a disease in an individual.
The **Incubation Period** determines the duration of quarantine for a disease.
**Herd Immunity** is the resistance of a group to the spread of an infectious agent because a high proportion of the group is immune.
**Environmental Sanitation** is a form of Primary Prevention aimed at controlling the transmission cycle.
**Health Education** is the most potent tool in Health Promotion, targeting behavioral change.
**Screening tests** must be highly sensitive to ensure no cases are missed (Secondary Prevention).
**Rehabilitation** includes Medical (restoring function), Social (restoring family/community ties), and Vocational (restoring earning capacity) aspects.
**Case-finding** is a form of secondary prevention where patients are identified when they come to the clinic for other reasons.
**Notification of disease** is a legal requirement for certain 'Notifiable Diseases' to the health authorities.
The goal of **Disability Limitation** is to prevent a clinical 'impairment' from becoming a 'handicap'.
**Elimination** requires the maintenance of high immunization levels to prevent the re-introduction of the disease.
**Eradication** is the ultimate goal of public health; currently, only Smallpox (human) and Rinderpest (animal) are eradicated.
**Pharmacology** is defined as the study of substances that interact with living systems through chemical processes, especially by binding to regulatory molecules.
**Pharmacokinetics** refers to what the body does to the drug, encompassing absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (**ADME**).
**Pharmacodynamics** refers to what the drug does to the body, including the biochemical and physiological effects and mechanisms of action.
A **Drug** is any chemical substance that brings about a change in biological function through its chemical actions.
A **Pro-drug** is an inactive precursor chemical that is readily absorbed and distributed and must be converted to the active biological form inside the body.
An example of a **Pro-drug** is **Levodopa**, which is converted to the active neurotransmitter **Dopamine** by **DOPA decarboxylase**.
**Enalapril** is a **Pro-drug** that is hydrolyzed in the liver to its active form, **Enalaprilat**, to inhibit **ACE**.
A **Placebo** is a pharmacologically inactive substance given to a patient, often used in clinical trials to control for the psychological effects of receiving treatment.
The **Placebo Effect** is the measurable improvement in health or behavior not attributable to a medication but to the patient's belief in the treatment.
A **Prototype drug** is the lead agent in a drug class (e.g., **Morphine** for opioids, **Propranolol** for beta-blockers) used as a reference for comparing other drugs in that class.
**Orphan Drugs** are drugs developed specifically to treat rare medical conditions (orphan diseases) which would not be profitable to produce without government assistance.
**Essential Drugs** are those that satisfy the priority healthcare needs of the population, selected by the **WHO** based on disease prevalence, safety, and efficacy.
**First Pass Effect** (or first-pass metabolism) is the phenomenon where the concentration of a drug is greatly reduced before it reaches the systemic circulation.
The primary site of the **First Pass Effect** is the **Liver**, but it can also occur in the **Gut Wall** and **Lungs**.
**Bioavailability** is the fraction of an administered dose of unchanged drug that reaches the systemic circulation.
Drugs with a high **First Pass Effect** (like **Nitroglycerin**) must be administered via non-oral routes like **Sublingual** or **Intravenous** to be effective.
**Volume of Distribution (Vd)** is a theoretical volume that relates the amount of drug in the body to the concentration of drug in the plasma.
The formula for **Vd** is: Amount of drug in the body divided by the **Plasma Concentration** (Cp).
A low **Vd** indicates that the drug is largely confined to the **Vascular Compartment** (plasma).
A high **Vd** (exceeding total body water) indicates the drug is extensively sequestered in **Extravascular Tissues** (e.g., **Digoxin**, **Chloroquine**).
**Absorption** is the transfer of a drug from its site of administration to the bloodstream.
Factors affecting **Absorption** include the **Route of Administration**, **Drug Solubility**, and the **pH** of the environment.
**Distribution** is the process by which a drug reversibly leaves the bloodstream and enters the interstitium and the cells of the tissues.
**Plasma Protein Binding** (primarily to **Albumin**) limits the distribution of a drug because only the **Free Fraction** can cross membranes.
**Metabolism** (or **Biotransformation**) is the chemical alteration of a drug in the body, primarily aimed at making it more **Polar** and **Hydrophilic** for excretion.
**Phase I Reactions** include **Oxidation**, **Reduction**, and **Hydrolysis**, often involving the **Cytochrome P450** enzyme system.
**Phase II Reactions** involve **Conjugation** (e.g., **Glucuronidation**, **Sulfation**, **Acetylation**) to create highly water-soluble metabolites.
**Elimination** is the irreversible removal of drug from the body by all routes, including metabolism and excretion.
**Excretion** is the removal of the intact drug or its metabolites from the body, primarily via the **Kidneys** (urine) and **Bile** (feces).
**Renal Excretion** involves three processes: **Glomerular Filtration**, **Active Tubular Secretion**, and **Passive Tubular Reabsorption**.
**Clearance (CL)** is the volume of plasma from which the drug is completely removed per unit of time.
**Half-life (t1/2)** is the time required for the plasma concentration of a drug to decrease by 50%.
It takes approximately **4 to 5 Half-lives** for a drug to reach **Steady State** during constant dosing.
**Zero-Order Kinetics** means a constant amount of drug is eliminated per unit time, regardless of concentration (e.g., **Ethanol**, high-dose **Aspirin**).
**First-Order Kinetics** means a constant fraction of drug is eliminated per unit time; the rate is proportional to the plasma concentration.
**Bioequivalence** occurs when two drug products show no significant difference in the rate and extent of absorption of the active ingredient.
**Enterohepatic Recirculation** is the cycle where drugs are excreted in bile, reabsorbed from the intestine, and returned to the liver, prolonging drug action.
**Loading Dose** is an initial higher dose of a drug given to reach the therapeutic plasma concentration (Steady State) rapidly.
**Maintenance Dose** is the amount of drug administered at regular intervals to maintain a steady-state concentration in the therapeutic range.
**Therapeutic Window** is the range between the minimum effective concentration and the minimum toxic concentration of a drug.
**Pharmacodynamics** is the study of the molecular, biochemical, and physiological effects of drugs and their mechanisms of action (What the drug does to the body).
A **Receptor** is a specific protein molecule on the cell surface or within the cell that binds a ligand and initiates a biological response.
**Affinity** is the measure of the tightness with which a drug binds to its receptor.
**Intrinsic Activity** (or Efficacy) refers to the ability of a drug-receptor complex to produce a functional response.
**Potency** refers to the amount of drug (concentration or dose) required to produce an effect of a given intensity; it is often expressed as the **EC50**.
**EC50** is the concentration of a drug that produces 50% of its maximal effect.
**Efficacy** (Emax) is the maximal response produced by a drug when all receptors are occupied.
An **Agonist** is a drug that binds to a receptor and activates it to produce a biological response (has both affinity and intrinsic activity).
A **Full Agonist** produces the maximum possible response the system is capable of (Intrinsic activity = 1).
A **Partial Agonist** binds to a receptor but produces a submaximal response even at 100% receptor occupancy (Intrinsic activity between 0 and 1).
In the presence of a full agonist, a **Partial Agonist** can act as an **Antagonist** by competing for the same receptor sites.
An **Inverse Agonist** binds to the same receptor as an agonist but produces an effect opposite to that of the agonist; it reduces **Constitutive Activity**.
An **Antagonist** is a drug that binds to a receptor but does not activate it; it prevents the binding of agonists (Affinity but zero intrinsic activity).
**Competitive Antagonists** bind reversibly to the active site; their effect can be overcome by increasing the concentration of the agonist (shifts curve to the **Right**).
**Non-competitive Antagonists** bind irreversibly to the active site or to an allosteric site, reducing the maximal response (shifts curve **Down**).
**Chemical Antagonism** occurs when two substances combine in solution, neutralizing the drug (e.g., **Protamine** neutralizing **Heparin**).
**Physiological Antagonism** (Functional Antagonism) occurs when two agonists act on different receptors to produce opposite effects (e.g., **Histamine** vs **Epinephrine** on bronchi).
**Pharmacokinetic Antagonism** occurs when one drug reduces the concentration of another by altering its ADME (e.g., **Phenobarbital** inducing metabolism of **Warfarin**).
**Spare Receptors** exist when the maximal drug response is achieved at a dosage where not all receptors are occupied.
The **Therapeutic Index (TI)** is a ratio that compares the blood concentration at which a drug becomes toxic to the concentration at which it is effective (**TD50 / ED50**).
A **Narrow Therapeutic Index** indicates that the difference between a therapeutic dose and a toxic dose is small (e.g., **Lithium**, **Warfarin**, **Digoxin**).
**Tachyphylaxis** is a rapid decrease in the response to a drug after repeated doses over a short period.
**Tolerance** is a gradual decrease in responsiveness to a drug, requiring higher doses to achieve the same effect.
**Down-regulation** occurs when chronic exposure to an agonist leads to a decrease in the number of available receptors.
**Up-regulation** occurs when chronic exposure to an antagonist leads to an increase in the number of available receptors.
**G-Protein Coupled Receptors (GPCRs)** are the largest family of cell-surface receptors and involve secondary messengers like **cAMP** and **IP3/DAG**.
**Ionotropic Receptors** (Ligand-gated ion channels) mediate rapid synaptic transmission (e.g., **Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor**).
**Metabotropic Receptors** are receptors that act through metabolic pathways (e.g., **GPCRs**).
**Intracellular Receptors** bind lipid-soluble ligands like **Steroids** and **Thyroid Hormones** and regulate gene transcription.
**Enzyme-Linked Receptors** (e.g., **Insulin Receptor**) possess intrinsic enzymatic activity, often **Tyrosine Kinase**.
**Specificity** refers to the ability of a drug to bind to one specific receptor type.
**Selectivity** refers to the drug's ability to produce one effect over another, which is dose-dependent.
**Desensitization** is the process where receptors become less responsive to a ligand after prolonged exposure.
**Quantal Dose-Response Curves** plot the fraction of the population that responds to a given dose of a drug.
**Graded Dose-Response Curves** plot the magnitude of effect against the concentration of the drug.
**Summation** occurs when the combined effect of two drugs is equal to the sum of their individual effects (1+1=2).
**Synergism** occurs when the combined effect of two drugs is greater than the sum of their individual effects (1+1=3).
**Potentiation** is a type of synergism where a drug that has no effect on its own increases the effect of another drug (0+1=2).
**Median Effective Dose (ED50)** is the dose at which 50% of individuals exhibit the specified therapeutic effect.
**Median Toxic Dose (TD50)** is the dose required to produce a toxic effect in 50% of the population.
The **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)** is divided into the **Sympathetic** (thoracolumbar) and **Parasympathetic** (craniosacral) divisions.
**Cholinergic Receptors** respond to **Acetylcholine (ACh)** and are divided into **Muscarinic** and **Nicotinic** types.
**Muscarinic M1 Receptors** are found in the **CNS** and **Gastric Parietal Cells**; they utilize the **Gq** mechanism.
**Muscarinic M2 Receptors** are primarily located in the **Heart** (SA and AV nodes); they utilize the **Gi** mechanism to decrease heart rate.
**Muscarinic M3 Receptors** are located on **Smooth Muscles**, **Exocrine Glands**, and **Vascular Endothelium**; they utilize the **Gq** mechanism.
Activation of **M3 Receptors** in the eye causes **Miosis** (contraction of sphincter pupillae) and **Accommodation** (contraction of ciliary muscle).
Activation of **M3 Receptors** in the lungs leads to **Bronchoconstriction** and increased mucus secretion.
**Nicotinic Nm Receptors** are found at the **Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ)**; they are ligand-gated ion channels causing skeletal muscle contraction.
**Nicotinic Nn Receptors** are located in the **Autonomic Ganglia** and **Adrenal Medulla**, mediating transmission and adrenaline release.
**Adrenergic Receptors** respond to **Norepinephrine** and **Epinephrine** and are divided into **Alpha** and **Beta** types.
**Alpha-1 Receptors** are located on **Vascular Smooth Muscle**; they utilize the **Gq** protein pathway to cause **Vasoconstriction**.
**Alpha-1** activation in the eye causes **Mydriasis** (dilation) via contraction of the radial dilator pupillae muscle.
**Alpha-2 Receptors** are primarily **Presynaptic**; they utilize the **Gi** mechanism to inhibit the further release of **Norepinephrine**.
**Alpha-2** receptors are also found in **Pancreatic Beta Cells**, where their activation decreases **Insulin** secretion.
**Beta-1 Receptors** are predominantly in the **Heart**; they utilize the **Gs** mechanism to increase heart rate (**Chronotropy**) and contractility (**Inotropy**).
**Beta-1 Receptors** are also found in the **Juxtaglomerular Cells** of the kidney, where they stimulate **Renin** release.
**Beta-2 Receptors** are found in **Bronchial Smooth Muscle** and **Skeletal Muscle Blood Vessels**; they utilize the **Gs** mechanism to cause **Relaxation/Dilation**.
**Beta-2** activation in the liver promotes **Glycogenolysis** and **Gluconeogenesis**, increasing blood glucose levels.
**Beta-3 Receptors** are found in **Adipose Tissue** (mediating **Lipolysis**) and the **Detrusor Muscle** of the bladder (mediating relaxation).
The **Gq Protein Mechanism** involves activation of **Phospholipase C**, which cleaves PIP2 into **IP3** (increases intracellular Calcium) and **DAG** (activates Protein Kinase C).
The **Gs Protein Mechanism** involves activation of **Adenylyl Cyclase**, which increases **cAMP** levels, activating **Protein Kinase A**.
The **Gi Protein Mechanism** involves inhibition of **Adenylyl Cyclase**, leading to decreased **cAMP** levels.
**Acetylcholine** is synthesized from **Choline** and **Acetyl-CoA** by the enzyme **Choline Acetyltransferase (ChAT)**.
**Acetylcholine** is rapidly degraded in the synaptic cleft by the enzyme **Acetylcholinesterase (AChE)**.
**Norepinephrine** synthesis starts with **Tyrosine**, converted to **DOPA** by **Tyrosine Hydroxylase** (the rate-limiting step).
**Norepinephrine** is primarily removed from the synapse by **Reuptake 1** (NET) into the presynaptic terminal.
Intracellular **Norepinephrine** is degraded by **Monoamine Oxidase (MAO)**, while extracellular degradation is handled by **Catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT)**.
The **Adrenal Medulla** secretes approximately 80% **Epinephrine** and 20% **Norepinephrine** upon sympathetic stimulation.
**Parasympathetic** stimulation of the bladder (via **M3**) causes contraction of the **Detrusor** muscle and relaxation of the **Sphincter**, leading to voiding.
**Sympathetic** stimulation of the bladder (via **Beta-3** and **Alpha-1**) causes relaxation of the **Detrusor** and contraction of the **Sphincter**, facilitating urine storage.
**M3 Receptor** activation on vascular endothelium triggers the release of **Nitric Oxide (NO)**, causing vasodilation despite no direct parasympathetic innervation to most vessels.
**Dopamine D1 Receptors** (Gs) cause vasodilation in **Renal**, **Mesenteric**, and **Coronary** vascular beds.
**Autoreceptors** are receptors located on the presynaptic terminal that respond to the transmitter released by that terminal (e.g., **Alpha-2** for NE).
**Heteroreceptors** are presynaptic receptors that respond to different ligands than the ones released by that neuron.
**Cholinomimetics** are drugs that mimic the effects of ACh, either directly (agonists) or indirectly (AChE inhibitors).
**Sympathomimetics** are drugs that mimic the effects of catecholamines (e.g., **Phenylephrine**, **Albuterol**).
**Parasympatholytics** (e.g., **Atropine**) block muscarinic receptors, leading to effects like tachycardia and dry mouth.
**Sympatholytics** (e.g., **Propranolol**, **Prazosin**) block adrenergic receptors to reduce sympathetic tone.
The **Enteric Nervous System (ENS)** is a semi-autonomous part of the ANS located in the gastrointestinal tract walls.
**Baroreceptor Reflex** is a rapid negative feedback loop where high blood pressure leads to increased vagal tone (Parasympathetic) and decreased sympathetic tone to lower BP.
**Pathology** is the bridge between basic sciences and clinical medicine, focusing on the **structural**, **biochemical**, and **functional** changes in cells and tissues.
