**Hemoglobin F (HbF)** is fetal hemoglobin, consisting of **2 Alpha and 2 Gamma chains**, having a higher affinity for oxygen.
**Hemoglobin A2 (HbA2)** is a minor adult form (2-3%) consisting of **2 Alpha and 2 Delta chains**.
Each molecule of Hemoglobin can carry up to **4 molecules of Oxygen** (8 atoms).
**Iron** is essential for Hb formation; it is absorbed in the **Duodenum** and transported in the blood by **Transferrin**.
Iron is stored in cells (liver/marrow) as **Ferritin**; if storage capacity is exceeded, it forms **Hemosiderin**.
**Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV)** measures the average size of RBCs; normal range is **80-100 fL**.
**Microcytic Anemia** (MCV < 80) is most commonly caused by **Iron Deficiency Anemia**.
**Macrocytic Anemia** (MCV > 100) is caused by **Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency**.
**Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC)** measures the concentration of Hb in a given volume of packed RBCs (Normal: **32-36%**).
**Hypochromic Anemia** is characterized by a low **MCHC**, often seen in Iron Deficiency.
**Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH)** is the average amount of Hb per RBC (Normal: **27-32 pg**).
**Sickle Cell Anemia** is caused by a point mutation (Valine for Glutamic Acid) in the **Beta chain** of Hemoglobin (**HbS**).
**Thalassemia** is a genetic defect involving the reduced synthesis rate of either alpha or beta globin chains.
In **Iron Deficiency Anemia**, labs show **Low Ferritin**, **High TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity)**, and **Low Serum Iron**.
**Methemoglobin** is a form where iron is in the **Ferric (Fe3+)** state rather than Ferrous (Fe2+), making it unable to bind oxygen.
**Polycythemia Vera** is a pathological condition characterized by an excess of RBCs due to a genetic mutation (JAK2).
**Secondary Polycythemia** occurs as an adaptive response to **High Altitude** or chronic lung disease.
**Aplastic Anemia** involves bone marrow failure, resulting in **Pancytopenia** (low RBCs, WBCs, and platelets).
White Blood Cells (Leukocytes) are divided into **Granulocytes** and **Agranulocytes**.
**Granulocytes** include **Neutrophils**, **Eosinophils**, and **Basophils**.
**Agranulocytes** include **Lymphocytes** and **Monocytes**.
**Neutrophils** (60-70% of WBCs) are the 'first responders' to bacterial infection.
**Diapedesis** is the process by which WBCs squeeze through capillary pores to enter tissues.
**Chemotaxis** is the movement of WBCs toward a chemical source (e.g., bacterial toxins, C5a, LTB4).
**Opsonization** is the coating of a pathogen with antibodies or **C3b** to enhance phagocytosis.
**Leukocytosis** is an increase in total WBC count, often seen in acute infections.
**Leukopenia** is a decrease in WBC count, making the body highly susceptible to infections.
**Leukemia** is the uncontrolled production of abnormal WBCs, leading to bone marrow crowding and **Anemia/Thrombocytopenia**.
**Macrophages** are mature **Monocytes** that have entered the tissues; they are larger and more powerful phagocytes than neutrophils.
The **Reticuloendothelial System (RES)** (or Mononuclear Phagocyte System) includes monocytes, mobile macrophages, and fixed macrophages in tissues.
**Kupffer Cells** are specialized macrophages located in the **Liver Sinusoids**.
**Alveolar Macrophages** are the resident phagocytes in the **Lungs**.
**Microglia** are the resident macrophages of the **Central Nervous System**.
**First Line of Defense** in inflammation is the **Tissue Macrophage**.
**Second Line of Defense** is the **Neutrophil Invasion** into the inflamed area.
**Third Line of Defense** is the **Second Macrophage Invasion** (Monocyte migration).
**Fourth Line of Defense** is the increased production of granulocytes and monocytes by the **Bone Marrow**.
The **Walling-off effect** is the delay of bacterial spread via the formation of **Fibrinogen clots** in the inflammation area.
**Basophils** and **Mast Cells** release **Histamine**, **Heparin**, and **Bradykinin**, playing a key role in Type I hypersensitivity.
**Eosinophils** are specialized for defense against **Parasitic Infections** and are elevated in **Allergic Reactions** (Asthma).
Eosinophils release **Major Basic Protein (MBP)** to kill parasites (larvae).
**Pus** is a collection of necrotic tissue, dead neutrophils, and dead macrophages.
**Natural Killer (NK) Cells** are a type of lymphocyte that kills virally infected and tumor cells without prior sensitization.
The **ABO Blood System** is based on the presence of **Antigens (Agglutinogens)** on the RBC surface: A and B.
**Type A** blood has A-antigens and **Anti-B antibodies**.
**Type B** blood has B-antigens and **Anti-A antibodies**.
**Type AB** blood has both A and B antigens and **No antibodies** (Universal Recipient).
**Type O** blood has **No antigens** and both Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies (Universal Donor).
**Landsteiner’s Law** states that if an agglutinogen is present on an RBC, the corresponding agglutinin (antibody) must be absent from the plasma.
**Agglutinins** (Antibodies) of the ABO system are mostly **IgM** and do not cross the placenta.
The **Rh System** is based on the **D-antigen**; people are classified as **Rh-positive** if they have the D-antigen.
Unlike ABO, **Anti-Rh antibodies** are NOT naturally occurring; they only develop after exposure to Rh-positive blood.
**Erythroblastosis Fetalis** (Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn) occurs when an **Rh-negative mother** carries an **Rh-positive fetus**.
In Erythroblastosis Fetalis, the mother's **Anti-D IgG** antibodies cross the placenta and attack fetal RBCs.
**RhoGAM** (anti-D immunoglobulin) is given to Rh-negative mothers to prevent sensitization to the D-antigen.
An **Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction** involves immediate agglutination and hemolysis of donor RBCs, leading to **Jaundice** and **Renal Failure**.
**Agglutination** is the clumping of RBCs, while **Hemolysis** is the actual rupture of the RBC membrane.
The **D-antigen** is the most immunogenic of the Rh group.
Blood typing involves mixing RBCs with known **Antisera** (Anti-A, Anti-B, Anti-D) to observe clumping.
**Cross-matching** is the final check before transfusion where donor RBCs are mixed with recipient serum.
**Hydrops Fetalis** is the most severe form of Erythroblastosis Fetalis, characterized by extreme fetal edema.
**Kernicterus** is brain damage in neonates caused by high levels of **Bilirubin** crossing the blood-brain barrier.
**Type O Negative** is the true universal donor blood because it lacks A, B, and D antigens.
**Anemia** is defined as a deficiency of **hemoglobin** in the blood, which can be caused by either too few **erythrocytes** or too little hemoglobin in the cells.
Morphological classification is based on **Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV)** and **Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC)**.
**Microcytic Anemia** is characterized by an **MCV < 80 fL**, typically seen in **Iron Deficiency Anemia**.
**Macrocytic (Megaloblastic) Anemia** features an **MCV > 100 fL**, often due to **Vitamin B12** or **Folic Acid** deficiency.
**Normocytic Anemia** presents with a normal **MCV (80-100 fL)** and is common in **acute blood loss** or **Anemia of Chronic Disease**.
**Hypochromic Anemia** occurs when **MCHC** is low, indicating a reduced concentration of hemoglobin per RBC, often giving cells a pale appearance.
**Aplastic Anemia** results from **bone marrow failure** due to radiation, chemotherapy, or toxic chemicals like **benzene**.
**Hemolytic Anemia** involves the premature destruction of RBCs; examples include **Hereditary Spherocytosis** and **Sickle Cell Anemia**.
In **Sickle Cell Anemia**, a point mutation causes **Valine** to replace **Glutamic Acid** in the beta-globin chain.
**Erythroblastosis Fetalis** is a hemolytic anemia of the newborn caused by **Rh incompatibility**.
The physiological effect of anemia is a decrease in **blood viscosity**, which reduces peripheral resistance.
Reduced oxygen-carrying capacity in anemia leads to **tissue hypoxia** and subsequent **vasodilation**.
Anemia causes a significant increase in **Cardiac Output** to maintain oxygen delivery to tissues.
One of the major effects of severe anemia is **increased workload on the heart**, which can lead to **acute heart failure**.
**Exercise intolerance** in anemic patients occurs because the heart cannot further increase its output to meet metabolic demands.
**Pica**, the craving for non-nutritive substances (like ice or dirt), is a clinical sign specifically associated with **Iron Deficiency**.
**Glossitis** (smooth tongue) and **Koilonychia** (spoon-shaped nails) are physical findings in chronic iron deficiency.
**Neurological symptoms**, including paresthesia and loss of vibratory sense, are hallmark signs of **Vitamin B12 deficiency** (**Pernicious Anemia**).
**Reticulocyte count** is an essential lab value to differentiate between decreased RBC production and increased RBC destruction.
A low reticulocyte count in the presence of anemia suggests **bone marrow suppression**.
A high reticulocyte count suggests **hemolysis** or response to **acute hemorrhage**.
**Serum Ferritin** is the most sensitive lab test for detecting **iron store depletion**.
**Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC)** is typically **increased** in iron deficiency anemia.
**Transferrin saturation** is calculated as (Serum Iron / TIBC) x 100; it is low in iron deficiency.
**Schistocytes** (fragmented RBCs) on a peripheral smear indicate **Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia** (MAHA).
**Hypersegmented neutrophils** are a classic finding on a blood smear for **Megaloblastic Anemia**.
**Haptoglobin** levels decrease in **intravascular hemolysis** as it binds to free hemoglobin.
**Unconjugated bilirubin** increases in hemolytic anemia, potentially leading to **jaundice**.
**Erythropoietin (EPO)** levels increase in most anemias (except renal failure) as a compensatory mechanism.
**Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH)** measures the average weight of hemoglobin per RBC.
**Thalassemia** is a genetic disorder of **globin chain synthesis** (alpha or beta) resulting in microcytic anemia.
**Fanconi Anemia** is a hereditary form of **Aplastic Anemia** associated with DNA repair defects.
**Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)** is caused by a deficiency in **GPI-anchor proteins** (CD55/CD59).
Chronic blood loss, such as from **occult GI bleeding**, is a leading cause of iron deficiency in adults.
**Anemia of Chronic Disease** is mediated by **Hepcidin**, which sequesters iron in macrophages.
**Sideroblastic Anemia** is characterized by the presence of **ringed sideroblasts** in the bone marrow (iron in mitochondria).
**G6PD Deficiency** causes oxidative stress in RBCs, leading to **Heinz bodies** and **Bite cells**.
**Warm Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia** is typically mediated by **IgG** antibodies.
**Cold Agglutinin Disease** is typically mediated by **IgM** antibodies.
The primary treatment for **Pernicious Anemia** is lifelong **intramuscular Vitamin B12** injections.
**Polycythemia** is defined as an abnormal increase in the number of circulating **Red Blood Cells**.
**Primary Polycythemia** (Polycythemia Vera) is a **myeloproliferative neoplasm** usually caused by a mutation in the **JAK2 gene**.
**Secondary Polycythemia** (Erythrocytosis) is a physiological response to **hypoxia**, increasing EPO production.
Common causes of secondary polycythemia include **high altitude**, **chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)**, and **heart failure**.
**Relative Polycythemia** is not an increase in RBC mass but a decrease in **plasma volume** (e.g., due to **dehydration** or **diuretics**).
In **Polycythemia Vera**, the **hematocrit** can rise to 60-70%.
A key lab finding in Polycythemia Vera is **low serum Erythropoietin (EPO)** because of feedback inhibition.
In secondary polycythemia due to hypoxia, **serum Erythropoietin** levels are **high**.
The primary effect of polycythemia on the body is a drastic increase in **blood viscosity**.
Increased viscosity leads to **sluggish blood flow**, which can impair oxygen delivery despite high RBC count.
Patients with polycythemia are at a high risk for **thromboembolic events** (strokes, MI, DVT) due to stasis.
**Aquagenic pruritus** (itching after a warm bath) is a classic symptom of **Polycythemia Vera**.
**Plethora**, a ruddy or cyanotic appearance of the skin, is caused by the engorgement of subpapillary venous plexuses.
Polycythemia causes an increase in **Total Blood Volume**, sometimes up to twice the normal amount.
**Hypertension** occurs in polycythemia due to the increased pressure required to pump viscous blood.
**Splenomegaly** is a common finding in Primary Polycythemia due to extra-medullary hematopoiesis or sequestration.
**Hyperuricemia** and **Gout** can occur in Polycythemia Vera due to high cell turnover.
**Gaisböck Syndrome** is a form of relative polycythemia seen in stressed, hypertensive, obese middle-aged men.
The **oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve** may shift in polycythemia to compensate for tissue needs.
Treatment for Polycythemia Vera includes **therapeutic phlebotomy** to keep hematocrit below 45%.
**Hydroxyurea** is a chemotherapeutic agent used to reduce cell counts in primary polycythemia.
**Low-dose Aspirin** is often prescribed to reduce the risk of thrombosis in polycythemic patients.
**Renal Cell Carcinoma** can cause secondary polycythemia via ectopic production of **Erythropoietin**.
Chronic **Carbon Monoxide** exposure (e.g., in smokers) causes secondary polycythemia by creating **carboxyhemoglobin**.
**Blood Doping** is an artificial cause of polycythemia used to enhance athletic performance.
In polycythemia, the **venous return** is often decreased because of the high resistance of viscous blood.
**Headache**, **dizziness**, and **visual disturbances** are common neurological symptoms of hyperviscosity.
**Hemorrhage** can paradoxically occur in polycythemia due to dysfunctional platelets or **acquired von Willebrand disease**.
The **JAK2 V617F mutation** makes hematopoietic stem cells hypersensitive to growth factors like EPO.
Primary polycythemia can eventually progress to **Myelofibrosis** or **Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)**.
**Hemostasis** is the process of preventing blood loss from a damaged vessel.
The four main stages of hemostasis are **Vascular Constriction**, **Platelet Plug Formation**, **Blood Coagulation**, and **Fibrous Tissue Growth**.
**Vascular Constriction** (Vasospasm) is the immediate response to vessel injury, mediated by **local myogenic spasm**.
**Endothelin-1** is a potent vasoconstrictor released by damaged endothelial cells.
**Nociceptive impulses** from the site of injury trigger reflex vasoconstriction via the nervous system.
Vasoconstriction is more effective when the vessel is crushed rather than cleanly cut.
The second stage is the formation of a **Platelet Plug**, which is sufficient to seal small vascular nicks.
**Primary Hemostasis** refers to the formation of the initial platelet plug.
**Secondary Hemostasis** refers to the activation of the **clotting cascade** and formation of **fibrin**.
**Tertiary Hemostasis** (Fibrinolysis) involves the eventual dissolution of the clot by **plasmin**.
**Prostacyclin (PGI2)** and **Nitric Oxide (NO)** are produced by healthy endothelium to inhibit hemostasis and keep blood fluid.
The **subendothelial matrix**, containing **collagen**, is exposed during injury, triggering the hemostatic process.
**Von Willebrand Factor (vWF)** acts as a bridge between exposed collagen and platelet receptors.
**Thromboxane A2 (TXA2)** is released by activated platelets to cause further vasoconstriction and platelet recruitment.
**Clot Retraction** occurs within 20 to 60 minutes after the clot is formed, squeezing out **serum**.
**Serum** is defined as plasma minus the clotting factors (fibrinogen, etc.).
**Platelets** contain contractile proteins **actin** and **myosin**, which are essential for clot retraction.
The **extrinsic pathway** of coagulation is triggered by **Tissue Factor (Factor III)**.
The **intrinsic pathway** is triggered by contact with **collagen** or a negatively charged surface.
Both pathways converge on the **Common Pathway** at the activation of **Factor X**.
**Thrombin (Factor IIa)** is the key enzyme that converts **Fibrinogen** to **Fibrin**.
**Factor XIII (Fibrin Stabilizing Factor)** creates covalent cross-links between fibrin strands for a stable clot.
**Calcium (Factor IV)** is required for almost all steps of the coagulation cascade.
The **endothelial lining** provides a physical barrier and secretes **heparin-like molecules** to prevent unwanted clotting.
Failure of hemostasis results in **hemorrhage**; overactive hemostasis results in **thrombosis**.
**Vitamin K** is essential for the synthesis of several clotting factors in the liver.
**Platelet count** and **Bleeding Time** are common tests used to evaluate primary hemostasis.
**Prothrombin Time (PT)** and **Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT)** evaluate secondary hemostasis.
**Fibrin degradation products (FDPs)** and **D-dimer** are markers of fibrinolysis.
**Thrombomodulin** is an endothelial receptor that binds thrombin, shifting its function from pro-clotting to activating **Protein C**.
**Platelets** (Thrombocytes) are minute, disc-shaped cell fragments derived from **Megakaryocytes** in the bone marrow.
The normal concentration of platelets in the blood is **150,000 to 450,000 per microliter**.
Platelets lack a nucleus and cannot reproduce; their average lifespan is **8 to 12 days**.
The cytoplasm contains **alpha granules** and **dense granules** (delta granules).
**Alpha granules** contain **vWF**, **Fibrinogen**, and **Platelet-Derived Growth Factor (PDGF)**.
**Dense granules** contain **ADP**, **ATP**, **Calcium**, and **Serotonin**.
Platelet membranes contain **glycoproteins** (e.g., **GP Ib**, **GP IIb/IIIa**) that prevent adherence to normal endothelium but allow adherence to injured areas.
The **GP Ib receptor** binds to **von Willebrand Factor** on exposed collagen (Platelet Adhesion).
Platelet **Activation** involves a shape change (forming pseudopods) and degranulation.
**Platelet Aggregation** is the binding of platelets to each other, primarily mediated by **Fibrinogen** linking **GP IIb/IIIa** receptors.
**ADP** and **Thromboxane A2** are potent stimuli for platelet aggregation.
**Cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1)** is the enzyme in platelets responsible for the synthesis of **Thromboxane A2**.
**Aspirin** irreversibly inhibits COX-1, thereby inhibiting platelet function for the duration of the platelet's life.
**Platelet-Derived Growth Factor (PDGF)** stimulates the repair of damaged vascular walls by inducing fibroblast proliferation.
Old or damaged platelets are primarily removed from circulation by **macrophages in the spleen**.
**Thrombopoietin** is the primary hormone regulating platelet production, produced mainly by the **liver**.
**Open Canalicular System** is a system of invaginations in the platelet membrane that facilitates the release of granule contents.
The **Dense Tubular System** in platelets sequesters **Calcium**, which is released during activation.
**Serotonin** released by platelets acts as a local vasoconstrictor.
**Bernard-Soulier Syndrome** is a genetic deficiency of the **GP Ib** receptor.
**Glanzmann Thrombasthenia** is a genetic deficiency of the **GP IIb/IIIa** receptor.
**Platelet Factor 4** is a protein released by platelets that neutralizes heparin-like molecules.
Platelet surfaces provide the necessary **phospholipids** (Platelet Factor 3) for the assembly of coagulation factor complexes.
The **contractile proteins** (actin, myosin, and thrombosthenin) allow the platelet plug to contract and pull the vessel edges together.
**Ristocetin cofactor assay** is a lab test used to evaluate the interaction between vWF and GP Ib.
High levels of **cAMP** within the platelet inhibit activation; **ADP** lowers cAMP to promote activation.
**P2Y12** is the specific ADP receptor on platelets targeted by drugs like **Clopidogrel**.
**Abciximab** is a monoclonal antibody that inhibits the **GP IIb/IIIa** receptor.
Platelets play a role in **inflammation** by releasing cytokines and interacting with leukocytes.
**Pseudothrombocytopenia** is a lab artifact caused by platelet clumping in **EDTA** tubes.
**Clotting Factors** are mostly inactive proteases (proenzymes) synthesized in the **liver**.
**Factor I** is **Fibrinogen**; **Factor II** is **Prothrombin**; **Factor III** is **Tissue Factor** (Thromboplastin).
**Factor IV** is **Calcium ions**, which are required for all clotting steps except the initial two of the intrinsic pathway.
**Factor V** (Proaccelerin) and **Factor VIII** (Antihemophilic factor) serve as **cofactors**, not enzymes.
The **Intrinsic Pathway** begins with the activation of **Factor XII** (Hageman factor) upon contact with collagen.
The sequence of the intrinsic pathway is **XII -> XI -> IX -> VIII**.
The **Extrinsic Pathway** is much faster (seconds) and is triggered by the release of **Tissue Factor (TF)** from damaged tissue.
Tissue Factor forms a complex with **Factor VII** to activate **Factor X**.
The **Common Pathway** starts with the activation of **Factor X** (Stuart-Prower factor).
**Prothrombin Activator** is a complex consisting of **Xa, Va, Calcium, and Phospholipids**.
**Prothrombin Activator** catalyzes the conversion of **Prothrombin (II)** to **Thrombin (IIa)**.
**Thrombin** acts as a polymerase, converting soluble **Fibrinogen (I)** into insoluble **Fibrin monomers**.
**Factor XIII (Fibrin Stabilizing Factor)** is activated by thrombin and cross-links fibrin to form a stable polymer.
**Vitamin K-dependent factors** include **II, VII, IX, X**, as well as **Protein C and S**.
**Warfarin** (Coumadin) acts by inhibiting **Vitamin K Epoxide Reductase**, preventing the gamma-carboxylation of these factors.
**Heparin** acts by significantly enhancing the activity of **Antithrombin III**, which inactivates Thrombin and Factor Xa.
**Antithrombin III** is a natural anticoagulant that removes thrombin from the blood.
**Protein C** and **Protein S** are natural anticoagulants that inactivate **Factors Va and VIIIa**.
**Prothrombin Time (PT)** measures the **Extrinsic** and Common pathways; it is used to monitor **Warfarin** therapy.
**International Normalized Ratio (INR)** is a standardized PT ratio used to ensure consistency across labs.
**Activated Partial Thromboplastin Time (aPTT)** measures the **Intrinsic** and Common pathways; it monitors **Heparin** therapy.
**Sodium Citrate** and **EDTA** are anticoagulants used in the lab; they work by **chelating (binding) Calcium**.
**Oxalate** is another lab anticoagulant that precipitates calcium.
The **Endothelial Glycocalyx** is a layer of mucopolysaccharides that repels clotting factors and platelets.
**Thrombomodulin**, bound to the endothelium, removes thrombin from circulation and activates **Protein C**.
**Tissue Plasminogen Activator (tPA)** is released by injured tissues to initiate **fibrinolysis**.
**Plasmin** is the active enzyme that digests fibrin fibers and other clotting factors.
**Alpha-2-antiplasmin** is a circulating inhibitor that prevents excessive plasmin activity.
**Factor VIII** circulates in the blood bound to **von Willebrand Factor** to prevent its premature degradation.
Deficiency of **Factor VIII** results in **Hemophilia A**; deficiency of **Factor IX** results in **Hemophilia B** (Christmas Disease).
**Excessive bleeding** can result from platelet disorders, coagulation factor deficiencies, or vascular wall defects.
**Vitamin K Deficiency** leads to bleeding because the liver cannot produce functional Factors II, VII, IX, and X.
Vitamin K deficiency is common in **newborns** (sterile gut) and in cases of **fat malabsorption** (bile duct obstruction).
**Hemophilia A** is an X-linked recessive deficiency of **Factor VIII**.
**Hemophilia B** (Christmas Disease) is an X-linked recessive deficiency of **Factor IX**.
Hemophilia is characterized by **deep tissue bleeding**, **hemarthrosis** (bleeding into joints), and prolonged aPTT with normal PT.
**Von Willebrand Disease (vWD)** is the most common inherited bleeding disorder, involving a defect in vWF.
vWD affects both **platelet adhesion** and the stability of **Factor VIII**, leading to mucosal bleeding and bruising.
**Thrombocytopenia** is defined as a platelet count **below 100,000/µL**.
Spontaneous bleeding (petechiae and purpura) usually does not occur until the platelet count falls below **20,000/µL**.
**Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP)** is an autoimmune condition where antibodies (IgG) target and destroy platelets.
**Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)** involves a deficiency in the **ADAMTS13** enzyme, leading to large vWF multimers and microthrombi.
**Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)** is a paradox where widespread clotting depletes factors, leading to massive bleeding.
Common triggers for DIC include **Sepsis**, **Trauma**, **Obstetric complications**, and **Malignancy**.
Lab findings in **DIC** include increased **PT**, **aPTT**, **D-dimer**, and decreased **Fibrinogen** and **Platelets**.
**Liver Disease** causes bleeding because the liver is the primary site for clotting factor synthesis.
**Uremia** (renal failure) causes qualitative platelet dysfunction (platelets don't aggregate well).
**Petechiae** (small red/purple dots) are characteristic of **platelet** disorders, not factor deficiencies.
**Purpura** refers to larger areas of bruising/bleeding under the skin.
**Ecchymosis** is the medical term for a large bruise.
**Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP)** is a vascular disorder (vasculitis) that causes bleeding into the skin and joints.
**Scurvy** (Vitamin C deficiency) causes bleeding due to defective **collagen** synthesis in vessel walls.
**Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)** is an immune reaction where antibodies against Heparin-PF4 complex activate platelets, causing thrombosis and low platelet counts.
**Factor V Leiden** is a genetic mutation that causes **thrombophilia** (excessive clotting) by making Factor V resistant to inactivation by Protein C.
**Antiphospholipid Syndrome** is an autoimmune condition causing a high risk of arterial and venous thrombosis.
Bleeding from **mucous membranes** (epistaxis, menorrhagia) points towards **Primary Hemostasis** defects (platelets/vWF).
**Desmopressin (DDAVP)** is used to treat mild Hemophilia A and vWD by releasing stored Factor VIII and vWF.
**Cryoprecipitate** is a blood product containing high concentrations of Fibrinogen, vWF, and Factor VIII.
**Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP)** contains all coagulation factors and is used to reverse Warfarin or treat liver disease bleeding.
**Vitamin K1 (Phytonadione)** administration takes 6-12 hours to begin producing new factors.
**Immunity** is the ability of the body to resist almost all types of organisms or toxins that tend to damage the tissues and organs.
**Innate Immunity** is the non-specific, first line of defense present from birth.
Components of innate immunity include **skin barrier**, **stomach acid**, **lysozyme in tears**, and **phagocytic cells** (neutrophils, macrophages).
**Acquired (Adaptive) Immunity** is highly specific and involves the memory of previous exposures.
**Humoral Immunity** is mediated by **B-lymphocytes** producing **antibodies**.
**Cell-Mediated Immunity** is mediated by **T-lymphocytes** and is effective against intracellular pathogens and cancer cells.
**Antigens** are substances (usually proteins or polysaccharides) that can elicit an immune response; they have specific sites called **epitopes**.
**Immunoglobulins** (antibodies) consist of two **heavy chains** and two **light chains** connected by disulfide bonds.
The **Variable (V) region** of the antibody determines its antigen specificity.
The **Constant (C) region** (Fc portion) determines the biological activity and class of the antibody.
**IgG** is the most abundant antibody (75%), can cross the **placenta**, and provides long-term immunity.
**IgM** is a **pentamer**, is the first antibody produced in the primary immune response, and is very effective at activating complement.
**IgA** is found in **secretions** (saliva, milk, mucus) and provides mucosal immunity.
**IgE** is involved in **allergic reactions** (binds to mast cells) and protection against **parasitic infections**.
**IgD** is found on the surface of B cells and functions as an antigen receptor.
**Helper T-cells (CD4+)** are the 'master switches' of the immune system; they secrete **lymphokines** (cytokines).
**IL-2** is a key lymphokine secreted by Helper T-cells that stimulates the growth and proliferation of Cytotoxic T-cells and B-cells.
**Cytotoxic T-cells (CD8+)** directly kill infected or cancerous cells by releasing **perforins** and **granzymes**.
**Memory B-cells** remain in the lymphoid tissue to provide a rapid and potent **Secondary Immune Response** upon re-exposure.
The **Secondary Response** is faster, has a higher magnitude, and lasts longer than the Primary Response.
**Antibodies** act by **neutralization** (masking toxic sites), **agglutination** (clumping), **precipitation**, and **lysis**.
The **Complement System** is a cascade of ~20 proteins that enhances the ability of antibodies and phagocytes to clear pathogens.
The **Classical Pathway** of complement is activated by **Antigen-Antibody complexes** (IgG or IgM).
The **Alternative Pathway** of complement is activated directly by microbial surface molecules (like LPS).
**C3b** is an important **opsonin** that coats bacteria to facilitate phagocytosis.
The **Membrane Attack Complex (MAC)**, consisting of C5b-C9, creates holes in bacterial cell membranes, causing lysis.
**C3a and C5a** are **anaphylatoxins** that trigger mast cell degranulation and attract neutrophils (chemotaxis).
**MHC Class I** molecules are found on all nucleated cells and present antigens to **CD8+ T-cells**.
**MHC Class II** molecules are found only on **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** and present antigens to **CD4+ T-cells**.
**Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** include **Macrophages**, **B-lymphocytes**, and **Dendritic cells**.
**Immune Tolerance** is the state of unresponsiveness of the immune system to substances that have the potential to induce an immune response.
**Self-tolerance** is the ability of the immune system to recognize and not attack the body's own tissues.
**Central Tolerance** occurs during lymphocyte development in the **Thymus** (for T-cells) and **Bone Marrow** (for B-cells).
**Clonal Selection** (Negative Selection) is the process where lymphocytes that react strongly to self-antigens are destroyed by **apoptosis**.
**Positive Selection** ensures that T-cells can recognize the body's MHC molecules.
**Peripheral Tolerance** occurs in the lymph nodes and tissues to suppress any self-reactive cells that escaped central selection.
**Anergy** is a form of peripheral tolerance where a lymphocyte becomes non-responsive due to a lack of co-stimulatory signals.
**Regulatory T-cells (Tregs)** play a crucial role in maintaining tolerance by suppressing the activity of other T-cells.
**Failure of Tolerance** leads to **Autoimmune Diseases**.
Mechanisms of tolerance failure include **Molecular Mimicry**, where a foreign antigen resembles a self-antigen (e.g., Rheumatic Fever).
Release of **sequestered antigens** (e.g., in the eye or testes) after trauma can lead to an autoimmune attack.
**Genetic factors**, such as specific **HLA alleles** (e.g., HLA-B27), increase the risk of failing tolerance.
**Environmental triggers** like viruses or drugs can modify self-antigens, making them appear foreign.
**Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)** is a classic autoimmune disease involving a loss of B-cell tolerance to nuclear antigens.
**Type 1 Diabetes** involves the failure of tolerance leading to T-cell-mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells.
**Rheumatoid Arthritis** involves the production of **Rheumatoid Factor** (an antibody against IgG).
**Myasthenia Gravis** is caused by autoantibodies against the **Acetylcholine receptor** at the neuromuscular junction.
**Graves' Disease** is caused by antibodies that stimulate the **TSH receptor**.
The **AIRE gene** is essential for expressing self-antigens in the thymus for negative selection; its defect causes polyendocrine autoimmunity.
Tolerance can be induced artificially (e.g., in allergy desensitization) by repeated exposure to small doses of antigen.
**Active Immunization** involves the administration of an antigen (vaccine) to stimulate the body's own immune system to produce antibodies and memory cells.
**Passive Immunity** is the transfer of pre-formed antibodies (e.g., from mother to fetus via placenta/milk or via IVIG).
Passive immunity provides **immediate but temporary** protection because the body does not produce its own memory cells.
**Live-attenuated vaccines** (e.g., MMR) contain weakened pathogens that replicate in the host without causing disease.
**Inactivated vaccines** (e.g., Polio Salk) contain killed pathogens and usually require booster doses.
**Hypersensitivity** refers to an exaggerated or inappropriate immune response to an antigen.
**Type I Hypersensitivity** is an **Immediate/Atopic Allergy** mediated by **IgE** and **Mast cells**.
In Type I, IgE binds to **Fc receptors** on mast cells and basophils; re-exposure causes **degranulation** and **Histamine** release.
**Anaphylaxis** is a severe, systemic Type I reaction causing widespread vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and bronchoconstriction.
**Urticaria** (hives) is a localized Type I reaction in the skin characterized by red, itchy wheals.
**Hay Fever** (Allergic Rhinitis) is a Type I reaction in the nasal mucosa causing sneezing and lacrimation.
**Type IV Hypersensitivity** is **Delayed-Type Hypersensitivity (DTH)**, which is cell-mediated (T-cells), not antibody-mediated.
**Delayed reactions** take **24 to 72 hours** to develop (e.g., the **Tuberculin skin test**).
**Contact Dermatitis** (e.g., from Poison Ivy or Nickel) is a classic example of Type IV hypersensitivity.
The physiological basis of Type IV involves **Helper T-cells** releasing cytokines that recruit and activate **macrophages**.
**Atopy** is a genetic predisposition to develop Type I hypersensitivity reactions (high IgE levels).
**Asthma** is an atopic condition where the Type I reaction occurs in the lower airways.
**Leukotrienes** and **Prostaglandins** are 'late-phase' mediators released by mast cells that sustain the allergic response.
**Epinephrine** is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis because it causes bronchodilation and vasoconstriction.
**Desensitization (Allergy shots)** involves injecting increasing amounts of allergen to shift the response from IgE to **IgG4**.
The **ABO Blood Group** system is based on the presence of **A and B antigens** (agglutinogens) on the RBC surface.
**Type O** blood has neither A nor B antigens and is the **universal donor** (of cells).
**Type AB** blood has both antigens and is the **universal recipient** (of cells).
**Agglutinins** (antibodies) against A or B antigens are naturally occurring (IgM) and appear in the plasma after birth.
The **Rh System** is based on the **D antigen**. Rh-positive individuals have the D antigen; Rh-negative individuals do not.
Unlike ABO, Rh antibodies (anti-D) do not occur naturally; they only develop after an Rh-negative person is exposed to Rh-positive blood.
**Erythroblastosis Fetalis** (Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn) occurs when an **Rh-negative mother** carries an **Rh-positive fetus**.
Initial exposure (usually during first delivery) causes the mother to produce **anti-Rh IgG** antibodies.
In subsequent Rh-positive pregnancies, the maternal IgG crosses the **placenta** and attacks fetal RBCs.
Clinical features of Erythroblastosis Fetalis include **anemia**, **jaundice**, and **hydrops fetalis** (severe edema).
**Kernicterus** is permanent brain damage in the newborn caused by high levels of **unconjugated bilirubin** crossing the blood-brain barrier.
Treatment for the newborn includes **Exchange Transfusion** with Rh-negative blood to remove maternal antibodies and bilirubin.
**RhoGAM** (anti-D immunoglobulin) is given to Rh-negative mothers at 28 weeks and after delivery to prevent sensitization.
**ABO Incompatibility** can also cause mild hemolytic disease of the newborn, usually when a Type O mother has a Type A or B baby.
**Stored Blood Changes**: Over time, stored blood undergoes a 'storage lesion'.
In stored blood, **2,3-BPG** levels decrease, causing the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to shift to the **left** (higher affinity, less delivery).
**Potassium** levels increase in stored blood due to leakage from aging RBCs (risk of **hyperkalemia**).
Stored blood becomes more **acidic** (pH decreases) as cells continue to perform anaerobic glycolysis.
Levels of **ATP** decrease in stored RBCs, leading to a loss of cell membrane integrity and a change in shape from discocyte to **echinocyte**.
**Factors V and VIII** are labile and their activity decreases significantly in stored whole blood.
A **Mismatched Transfusion Reaction** occurs when recipient antibodies attack donor RBCs, causing **hemolysis**.
**Acute Hemolytic Reaction** is usually due to ABO incompatibility and occurs almost immediately.
The primary mechanism is **intravascular hemolysis** mediated by **Complement activation**.
Clinical features include **fever**, **chills**, **low back pain** (flank pain), and **dark urine** (hemoglobinuria).
**Hemoglobinuria** can lead to **Acute Renal Failure** due to the toxic effect of free hemoglobin and debris on renal tubules.
**Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)** can be triggered by the release of thromboplastic substances from lysed RBCs.
**Delayed Hemolytic Reaction** occurs days to weeks later, usually due to minor antigens (e.g., Kidd, Duffy) and is usually **extravascular**.
**Febrile Non-Hemolytic Reaction** is the most common reaction, caused by antibodies against donor **white blood cells** or cytokines.
**Allergic Reactions** during transfusion (urticaria) are caused by antibodies to donor **plasma proteins**.
**Transfusion Related Acute Lung Injury (TRALI)** is a serious complication caused by antibodies in donor plasma reacting with recipient neutrophils in the lungs.
**Transfusion Associated Circulatory Overload (TACO)** occurs when blood is infused too quickly, leading to **pulmonary edema**.
**Infectious hazards** of blood transfusion include transmission of **HIV**, **Hepatitis B**, **Hepatitis C**, and **Syphilis**.
**Iron Overload** (Hemosiderosis) can occur in patients receiving chronic transfusions (e.g., Thalassemia).
**Citrate toxicity** can occur in massive transfusions, leading to **hypocalcemia** because the citrate preservative binds recipient calcium.
**Transplantation of Tissues** involves the transfer of an **Autograft** (same person), **Isograft** (identical twin), **Allograft** (same species), or **Xenograft** (different species).
**Hyperacute Rejection** occurs within minutes, mediated by pre-existing antibodies in the recipient.
**Acute Rejection** occurs within days to weeks, primarily mediated by **T-cells** (cell-mediated immunity).
**Chronic Rejection** occurs over months to years, involving chronic inflammation and vascular fibrosis.
**Graft-Versus-Host Disease (GVHD)** occurs when immunocompetent cells in the graft (usually bone marrow) attack the recipient's tissues.
Successful transplantation requires matching of **HLA (Human Leukocyte Antigens)**.
**Tissue Typing** (HLA matching) is the process of comparing the **Major Histocompatibility Complex (MHC)** antigens of the donor and recipient.
The most important antigens to match for successful organ transplantation are **HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-DR**.
**Cross-matching** involves mixing recipient serum with donor lymphocytes to detect pre-existing antibodies.
**Mixed Lymphocyte Culture (MLC)** is a functional test to determine the degree of T-cell reactivity between donor and recipient.
**Prevention of Graft Rejection** is primarily achieved through **immunosuppressive therapy**.
**Glucocorticoids** (e.g., Prednisone) suppress the immune system by inhibiting cytokine gene expression and reducing lymphocyte counts.
**Cyclosporine** and **Tacrolimus** are **calcineurin inhibitors** that specifically block **IL-2** production by T-cells.
**Azathioprine** is an antimetabolite that inhibits DNA synthesis, thereby preventing the proliferation of rapidly dividing lymphocytes.
**Mycophenolate Mofetil (MMF)** is another antimetabolite that inhibits the synthesis of guanosine nucleotides in T and B cells.
**Sirolimus (Rapamycin)** inhibits the **mTOR pathway**, which blocks the response of T-cells to IL-2.
**Monoclonal Antibodies** (e.g., **Basiliximab**) can be used to target and block the **IL-2 receptor (CD25)**.
**Antithymocyte Globulin (ATG)** is a polyclonal antibody that depletes the recipient's T-cells.
The major side effect of chronic immunosuppression is an increased risk of **opportunistic infections** (e.g., CMV, fungal).
Immunosuppression also increases the long-term risk of **malignancies**, especially **lymphoma** and skin cancers.
**Tolerance Induction** in transplantation is a goal where the recipient accepts the graft without long-term drugs (e.g., via chimerism).
**Corneal transplants** have a high success rate because the cornea is **avascular** (an 'immunologically privileged' site).
In **Bone Marrow Transplantation**, the recipient's own immune system must first be destroyed ('myeloablation') by radiation or chemo.
**MHC Class I** molecules (A, B, C) are recognized by **CD8+ T-cells**; **MHC Class II** (DP, DQ, DR) by **CD4+ T-cells**.
The 'direct pathway' of rejection involves donor APCs presenting donor MHC to recipient T-cells.
The 'indirect pathway' involves recipient APCs processing donor MHC and presenting it to recipient T-cells.

nuggets.lo_heading: HL-A-001. Hematopoietic & Lymphoid Tissue - Components and Function
content: The **Bone Marrow** is the primary site of **Hematopoiesis** after the 5th month of fetal development.
**Red Bone Marrow** is actively hematopoietic and contains a high concentration of **Erythrocytes** and their precursors.
**Yellow Bone Marrow** is primarily composed of **Adipocytes** and serves as a reservoir, but can revert to red marrow during severe **Anemia**.
In adults, **Red Marrow** is restricted to the **Axial Skeleton**, including the skull, vertebrae, ribs, sternum, and proximal epiphyses of the femur and humerus.
The **Hematopoietic Stem Cell (HSC)** is characterized by the expression of the **CD34** surface marker.
**Primary Lymphoid Organs** include the **Bone Marrow** and the **Thymus**, where lymphocytes reach functional maturity.
**Secondary Lymphoid Organs** include the **Spleen**, **Lymph Nodes**, and **Mucosa-Associated Lymphoid Tissue (MALT)**.
**Lymph Nodes** are the only lymphoid organs that filter **Lymph** via **Afferent Lymphatic Vessels**.
The **Cortex** of a lymph node contains **Lymphoid Follicles**, which are primary sites for **B-lymphocyte** proliferation.
**Secondary Follicles** in lymph nodes contain **Germinal Centers**, indicating an active immune response and **Somatic Hypermutation**.
The **Paracortex** of the lymph node is the **T-cell dependent zone** and contains **High Endothelial Venules (HEV)** for lymphocyte extravasation.
**High Endothelial Venules (HEV)** express **Selectins** and **Addressins** to facilitate the entry of naive T-cells from the blood.
The **Medulla** of the lymph node consists of **Medullary Cords** (containing plasma cells) and **Medullary Sinuses**.
**Macrophages** in the lymph node sinuses act as filters to remove particulate matter and pathogens from the lymph.
The **Spleen** is the largest lymphoid organ and functions to filter **Blood** rather than lymph.
**White Pulp** of the spleen consists of the **Periarteriolar Lymphoid Sheath (PALS)**, which is rich in **T-cells**.
The **Marginal Zone** of the spleen is located between red and white pulp and captures **Blood-borne Antigens**.
**Red Pulp** of the spleen consists of **Splenic Sinoids** and **Splenic Cords (Cords of Billroth)**.
**Splenic Cords** contain a meshwork of reticular fibers filled with **Macrophages** that destroy aged **Erythrocytes**.
**Erythrocyte Extravasation** occurs in the red pulp; healthy RBCs deform to re-enter circulation, while rigid/old RBCs are phagocytosed.
**MALT** includes specialized tissues like **Peyer's Patches** in the ileum and **Tonsils** in the pharynx.
**Peyer's Patches** contain specialized **M-cells** (Microfold cells) that sample antigens from the intestinal lumen.
**B-lymphocytes** differentiate into **Plasma Cells** which produce antibodies (Immunoglobulins).
**T-lymphocytes** are classified into **CD4+ Helper T-cells** and **CD8+ Cytotoxic T-cells**.
**Natural Killer (NK) cells** are large granular lymphocytes that provide innate immunity against virally infected cells.
**Dendritic Cells** are the most potent **Antigen-Presenting Cells (APCs)** and migrate to lymph nodes to activate naive T-cells.
The **Stroma** of most lymphoid organs is composed of **Type III Collagen** (reticular fibers) produced by fibroblastic reticular cells.
The **Thymus** lacks reticular fibers; its stroma is formed by **Epithelioreticular Cells**.
**Lymphatic Capillaries** are more permeable than blood capillaries because they lack a continuous **Basal Lamina**.
The **Right Lymphatic Duct** drains lymph from the upper right quadrant of the body into the **Right Subclavian Vein**.
The **Thoracic Duct** drains lymph from the rest of the body into the junction of the **Left Internal Jugular** and **Left Subclavian Veins**.
**Chyle** is a milky lymph rich in **Chylomicrons** absorbed from the small intestine.
**Lymphadenopathy** refers to the enlargement of lymph nodes, often due to infection (tender) or malignancy (non-tender, fixed).
**Asplenia** (absence of spleen) increases susceptibility to **Encapsulated Bacteria** such as *Streptococcus pneumoniae*.
**Howell-Jolly Bodies** are nuclear remnants in RBCs typically removed by the spleen; their presence indicates **Hyposplenism**.
**Thymic Selection** ensures that T-cells are self-MHC restricted (Positive Selection) and self-tolerant (Negative Selection).
The **Blood-Thymus Barrier** prevents circulating antigens from reaching developing T-cells in the thymic cortex.
**DiGeorge Syndrome** involves thymic hypoplasia leading to profound **T-cell Deficiency**.
**Splenomegaly** can cause **Sequestration**, leading to **Thrombocytopenia** and **Anemia**.
**Lymphocyte Homing** is the process by which lymphocytes migrate to specific lymphoid tissues using **Chemokines** like CCL19 and CCL21.

nuggets.lo_heading: HL-A-001. Spleen - Location, Coverings, and Relations
content: The **Spleen** is located in the **Left Hypochondrium** and part of the **Epigastrium**.
The spleen lies deep to the **9th, 10th, and 11th Ribs** on the left side.
The **Long Axis** of the spleen is parallel to the **10th Rib**.
The **Spleen** is entirely surrounded by **Peritoneum** except at the **Hilum**.
The spleen is separated from the ribs by the **Diaphragm** and the **Left Costodiaphragmatic Recess**.
The **Average Dimensions** of the spleen are 1 inch thick, 3 inches wide, and 5 inches long (**Harris's Rule of 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11**).
The **Weight** of a healthy adult spleen is approximately **7 ounces** (150-200g).
The **Diaphragmatic Surface** of the spleen is convex and smooth, related to the undersurface of the diaphragm.
The **Visceral Surface** of the spleen is irregular and contains impressions from adjacent organs.
The **Gastric Impression** is the largest and most superior, located between the superior border and the hilum.
The **Renal Impression** is located between the hilum and the inferior border, related to the **Left Kidney**.
The **Colic Impression** is located at the lateral (anterior) pole, related to the **Left Colic Flexure** (Splenic Flexure).
The **Pancreatic Impression** is located between the hilum and the colic impression, related to the **Tail of the Pancreas**.
The **Superior Border** of the spleen is sharp and characteristically **Notched**, representing its lobulated fetal origin.
The **Inferior Border** of the spleen is rounded and smooth.
The **Anterior Pole** (Lateral end) is expanded and reaches the mid-axillary line.
The **Posterior Pole** (Medial end) is rounded and directed toward the vertebral column at the level of **T10**.
The **Gastrosplenic Ligament** connects the hilum of the spleen to the **Greater Curvature of the Stomach**.
The **Gastrosplenic Ligament** contains the **Short Gastric Vessels** and the **Left Gastro-omental Vessels**.
The **Splenorenal (Lienorenal) Ligament** connects the hilum of the spleen to the **Left Kidney**.
The **Splenorenal Ligament** contains the **Splenic Artery**, **Splenic Vein**, and the **Tail of the Pancreas**.
The **Phrenicocolic Ligament** is a peritoneal fold supporting the spleen from below, though not attached to it.
The **Spleen** is an **Intraperitoneal Organ**.
In cases of **Splenomegaly**, the spleen expands inferiorly and medially toward the **Right Iliac Fossa**.
A palpable **Splenic Notch** on physical exam helps distinguish the spleen from the left kidney.
The spleen moves with **Respiration** because it is closely related to the diaphragm.
The **Splenic Capsule** is composed of dense irregular connective tissue and some smooth muscle fibers.
**Traumatic Rupture** of the spleen is common due to its friable nature and proximity to the lower ribs.
**Kehr's Sign** is referred pain to the **Left Shoulder** caused by splenic rupture irritating the phrenic nerve.
The **Hilum** of the spleen is located on the visceral surface and serves as the entry/exit point for vessels.
The **Left Suprarenal Ampulla** is sometimes related to the spleen near the renal impression.
The **Spleen** develops from **Mesenchymal Cells** in the **Dorsal Mesogastrium**.
**Accessory Spleens** (Splenuli) are common (10% of population) and usually found near the hilum or the tail of the pancreas.
The spleen is located between the **Fundus of the Stomach** and the **Diaphragm**.
The **Subphrenic Space** (Left) lies superior to the spleen.
The **Left Pleurisy** or pneumonia can mimic splenic pain due to diaphragmatic irritation.
The **Spleen** is typically not palpable in adults unless it is enlarged to **2-3 times** its normal size.
The **Superior Border** separates the gastric impression from the diaphragmatic surface.
The **Inferior Border** separates the renal impression from the diaphragmatic surface.
The **Medial End** of the spleen lies approximately **4 cm** from the midline at the level of the T10 spine.

nuggets.lo_heading: HL-A-001. Splenic Artery - Origin, Course, and Distribution
content: The **Splenic Artery** is the largest of the three branches of the **Celiac Trunk**.
The **Celiac Trunk** originates from the **Abdominal Aorta** at the level of the **T12/L1** vertebrae.
The **Splenic Artery** follows a remarkably **Tortuous (Coiled)** course along the superior border of the **Pancreas**.
The tortuosity of the splenic artery allows for the distension of the **Stomach** and movement of the **Diaphragm**.
The artery runs **Retroperitoneally** behind the **Lesser Sac** (Omental Bursa) until it enters the splenorenal ligament.
The artery travels within the **Splenorenal Ligament** to reach the **Hilum of the Spleen**.
**Pancreatic Branches** (Rami pancreatici) include the **Greater Pancreatic Artery** (Arteria pancreatica magna) and the **Dorsal Pancreatic Artery**.
The **Short Gastric Arteries** (5-7 in number) arise near the hilum and supply the **Fundus of the Stomach**.
The **Left Gastro-omental (Gastroepiploic) Artery** arises near the hilum and runs along the **Greater Curvature** of the stomach.
The **Left Gastro-omental Artery** anastomoses with the **Right Gastro-omental Artery** (from the gastroduodenal artery).
Before entering the hilum, the splenic artery divides into **5 to 6 Terminal Branches**.
These terminal branches enter the spleen and divide into **Segmental Arteries**, creating **Vascular Segments** (no anastomoses between them).
Inside the spleen, segmental arteries branch into **Trabecular Arteries** that run within the connective tissue trabeculae.
**Central Arteries** (or Arterioles) arise from trabecular arteries and are surrounded by the **Periarteriolar Lymphoid Sheath (PALS)**.
The **Penicillar Arterioles** are the fine terminal branches of the central arteries in the red pulp.
The **Splenic Artery** provides the primary arterial supply to the **Body and Tail of the Pancreas**.
A **Splenic Artery Aneurysm** is the most common visceral artery aneurysm, often associated with pregnancy.
**Erosion** of a gastric ulcer on the posterior wall of the stomach can lead to massive hemorrhage from the **Splenic Artery**.
The **Posterior Gastric Artery** (present in 50% of people) arises from the mid-portion of the splenic artery.
The splenic artery is accompanied by the **Splenic Plexus** of nerves (sympathetic fibers).
The **Splenic Artery** lies superior to the **Splenic Vein** during its course along the pancreas.
The **Splenic Artery** is a major source of blood to the **Greater Omentum** via the left gastro-omental branch.
**Infatction** of splenic segments occurs easily because the terminal branches are **End Arteries**.
During a **Splenectomy**, the splenic artery must be ligated carefully to avoid damaging the **Tail of the Pancreas**.
The **Splenic Artery** provides a collateral pathway to the stomach if the **Left Gastric Artery** is occluded.
The **Short Gastric Arteries** have a very poor collateral network, making the gastric fundus susceptible to ischemia if they are ligated.
The **Arteria Pancreatica Magna** usually enters the body of the pancreas at the junction of its middle and left thirds.
The **Splenic Artery** is located posterior to the **Stomach** and **Lesser Sac**.
The **Splenic Artery** is located anterior to the **Left Kidney** and **Left Crus of the Diaphragm**.
The terminal divisions of the artery occur within the layers of the **Splenorenal Ligament**.
**Nutritional Blood Supply** to the splenic parenchyma is provided by the penicillar arterioles.
The **Central Artery** is actually eccentric in its position within the lymphoid follicle (White Pulp).
The **Splenic Artery** diameter is approximately **5-8 mm** in adults.
**Calcification** of the splenic artery is a common incidental finding on abdominal X-rays in elderly patients.
The splenic artery's tortuosity increases with **Age** and **Atherosclerosis**.
The **Left Gastro-omental Artery** gives off **Omental (Epiploic) Branches** to the greater omentum.
The **Splenic Artery** is an essential landmark in **CT Imaging** of the upper abdomen.
Ligation of the splenic artery distal to the pancreatic branches allows the pancreas to remain viable.
The splenic artery contributes to the **Vascularization** of the left side of the **Greater Omentum**.
The **Splenic Artery** is the third most common site of intra-abdominal aneurysm after the aorta and iliac arteries.

nuggets.lo_heading: HL-A-001. Venous Drainage of Spleen and Portal Vein Formation
content: The **Splenic Vein** is formed by several tributaries emerging from the **Hilum of the Spleen**.
The **Splenic Vein** runs a relatively **Straight** course, unlike the tortuous splenic artery.
The **Splenic Vein** lies **Inferior** to the splenic artery and **Posterior** to the body and tail of the pancreas.
The **Splenic Vein** receives the **Short Gastric Veins** and the **Left Gastro-omental Vein**.
The **Splenic Vein** also receives several **Pancreatic Veins** from the body and tail of the pancreas.
The **Inferior Mesenteric Vein (IMV)** usually drains into the **Splenic Vein** behind the body of the pancreas.
The **Portal Vein** is formed by the union of the **Splenic Vein** and the **Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV)**.
The union of the Splenic Vein and SMV occurs at the level of **L2**, posterior to the **Neck of the Pancreas**.
The **Portal Vein** is approximately **8 cm** long in adults.
The **Portal Vein** ascends behind the first part of the **Duodenum** to reach the **Porta Hepatis**.
In the **Lesser Omentum** (Hepatoduodenal Ligament), the portal vein lies **Posterior** to the Proper Hepatic Artery and Common Bile Duct.
The **Portal Vein** carries approximately **75%** of the blood flow to the liver, but only 50% of the oxygen supply.
The **Portal Vein** drains blood from the **Gastrointestinal Tract** (esophagus to rectum), **Spleen**, **Pancreas**, and **Gallbladder**.
The **Right and Left Gastric Veins** drain directly into the **Portal Vein**.
The **Cystic Vein** (from the gallbladder) usually drains into the **Right Branch** of the portal vein.
The **Paraumbilical Veins** run in the **Ligamentum Teres** and drain into the **Left Branch** of the portal vein.
**Portosystemic Anastomoses** occur where the portal venous system communicates with the systemic venous system.
The **Lower Esophagus** anastomosis is between the **Left Gastric Vein** (portal) and **Esophageal Branches of Azygos Vein** (systemic).
Clinical consequence of esophageal anastomosis: **Esophageal Varices**, which can lead to life-threatening hematemesis.
The **Anal Canal** anastomosis is between the **Superior Rectal Vein** (portal) and **Middle/Inferior Rectal Veins** (systemic).
Clinical consequence of anal anastomosis: **Internal Hemorrhoids**.
The **Umbilicus** anastomosis is between **Paraumbilical Veins** (portal) and **Superficial Epigastric Veins** (systemic).
Clinical sign of umbilical anastomosis: **Caput Medusae** (distended veins radiating from the umbilicus).
The **Retroperitoneal** anastomosis (of **Retzius**) involves veins of the colon (portal) and **Lumbar Veins** (systemic).
**Portal Hypertension** is defined as a portal venous pressure exceeding **10-12 mmHg**.
**Cirrhosis** of the liver is the most common cause of portal hypertension (intrahepatic obstruction).
**Splenic Vein Thrombosis** can cause **Isolated Gastric Varices** without affecting the rest of the portal system.
The **Portal Vein** lacks **Valves**, allowing blood to flow in a retrograde direction when pressure rises.
The **Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV)** drains the small intestine, cecum, ascending colon, and transverse colon.
The **Inferior Mesenteric Vein (IMV)** drains the descending colon, sigmoid colon, and upper rectum.
The **Splenic Vein** crosses the anterior surface of the **Left Kidney** and **Aorta** before joining the SMV.
The **Portal Vein** divides into **Right and Left Terminal Branches** at the porta hepatis.
The **Left Branch** of the portal vein is longer and more horizontal than the right branch.
The **Portal Vein** provides the liver with **Nutrients** absorbed from the gut.
**Pylephlebitis** is an infective thrombosis of the portal vein, often arising from appendicitis or diverticulitis.
The **Superior Pancreaticoduodenal Vein** usually joins the portal vein, while the inferior joins the SMV.
In **Budd-Chiari Syndrome**, there is an obstruction of the **Hepatic Veins**, leading to secondary portal hypertension.
The **Splenic Vein** is a key landmark for identifying the **Pancreas** on ultrasound.
The **Portal Vein** is formed by the junction of the **Splenic** and **SMV** veins, often referred to as the **Portal Confluence**.
Blood from the **SMV** and **Splenic Vein** tends to remain in **Laminar Streams** within the portal vein, often directing SMV blood to the right lobe of the liver.

nuggets.lo_heading: HL-A-001. Thymus - Location, Relations, and Age-Related Changes
content: The **Thymus** is a primary lymphoid organ located in the **Superior Mediastinum** and the anterior part of the **Inferior Mediastinum**.
In infants, the thymus may extend superiorly into the **Neck** as far as the **Thyroid Gland**.
The thymus lies **Posterior** to the **Manubrium** and the body of the sternum.
The thymus lies **Anterior** to the **Pericardium**, **Arch of the Aorta**, and the **Great Vessels**.
The **Great Vessels** related to the thymus include the **Brachiocephalic Veins** and the **Superior Vena Cava**.
The thymus consists of two highly asymmetrical **Lobes** (Right and Left) held together by connective tissue.
The **Thymus** is most active and reaches its maximum **Relative Size** during the **Neonatal Period**.
The thymus reaches its maximum **Absolute Weight** (approx. 30-40g) at **Puberty**.
After puberty, the thymus undergoes **Age Involution**, where lymphoid tissue is replaced by **Adipose (Fatty) Tissue**.
Despite involution, the thymus continues to produce **T-lymphocytes** at a reduced rate throughout adulthood.
The **Thymic Capsule** is a thin layer of connective tissue that sends **Septa** (trabeculae) into the organ, dividing it into **Lobules**.
Each thymic lobule consists of an outer, darkly staining **Cortex** and an inner, lighter-staining **Medulla**.
The **Thymic Cortex** is densely packed with **Immature T-lymphocytes (Thymocytes)**.
The **Thymic Medulla** contains **Hassall's Corpuscles** (Thymic Corpuscles), which are whorls of keratinized epithelial cells.
**Hassall's Corpuscles** are a diagnostic histological feature of the thymus.
The **Blood-Thymus Barrier** is present only in the **Cortex** to protect developing T-cells from premature antigen exposure.
The barrier consists of **Capillary Endothelium**, a thick **Basal Lamina**, and **Type I Epithelioreticular Cells**.
**Arterial Supply** to the thymus is primarily from the **Internal Thoracic Arteries** and **Inferior Thyroid Arteries**.
**Venous Drainage** is via the **Left Brachiocephalic**, **Internal Thoracic**, and **Inferior Thyroid Veins**.
The **Lymphatics** of the thymus are unique because they are **Only Efferent**; the thymus has no afferent lymphatics.
**Efferent Lymphatics** from the thymus drain into the **Parasternal**, **Brachiocephalic**, and **Tracheobronchial Lymph Nodes**.
**Nerve Supply** to the thymus is provided by the **Vagus Nerve** (parasympathetic) and **Sympathetic Trunks**.
The thymus is the site of **T-cell Maturation** and the development of **Self-Tolerance**.
**Positive Selection** occurs in the **Cortex**, where T-cells must recognize self-MHC molecules.
**Negative Selection** occurs in the **Medulla**, where T-cells that react too strongly to self-antigens are eliminated by **Apoptosis**.
**Thymic Hyperplasia** is often associated with **Myasthenia Gravis** (an autoimmune disorder affecting the NMJ).
A **Thymoma** is a tumor arising from the epithelial cells of the thymus; it may compress the **Trachea** or **Great Vessels**.
The thymus is derived embryologically from the **3rd Pharyngeal Pouch** (and sometimes the 4th).
**Inferior Parathyroid Glands** also develop from the 3rd pharyngeal pouch and may be found within the thymus.
The **Thymic Medulla** of all lobules is continuous through the central core of the lobes.
**Thymic Involution** is accelerated by **Stress** and high levels of **Corticosteroids**.
The thymus lacks **Lymphoid Follicles** and **B-cells** under normal non-pathological conditions.
**Type VI Epithelioreticular Cells** are the cells that form the Hassall's corpuscles.
The **Thymus** does not participate in filtering lymph; it is an inductive environment for lymphocyte production.
In the elderly, the thymus is often reduced to a small remnant of **Fibrofatty Tissue** that is difficult to distinguish from mediastinal fat.
The **Internal Thoracic Artery** (formerly Internal Mammary) runs lateral to the thymus.
The **Left Brachiocephalic Vein** passes horizontally behind the thymus in children.
**Pre-T cells** migrate from the **Bone Marrow** to the thymic cortex to begin differentiation.
The thymus is one of the first organs to develop an immune function in the fetus.
**DiGeorge Syndrome (22q11.2 deletion)** results in a failure of the 3rd/4th pouches to develop, causing **Thymic Aplasia** and hypocalcemia.

nuggets.lo_heading: HL-B-001. Hemoglobin and its types/ RBCs
content: The first step of **heme synthesis** involves the condensation of **succinyl CoA** and **glycine** to form **delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA)**.
The enzyme **ALA synthase (ALAS)** is the rate-limiting enzyme and requires **Pyridoxal Phosphate (Vitamin B6)** as a cofactor.
**ALAS1** is found in the liver and is inhibited by **hemin** (negative feedback), while **ALAS2** is erythroid-specific and regulated by **iron levels**.
The second step occurs in the cytosol where two molecules of ALA condense to form **porphobilinogen (PBG)** via the enzyme **ALA dehydratase**.
**ALA dehydratase** and **Ferrochelatase** are both extremely sensitive to inhibition by **lead**, leading to **lead poisoning** symptoms.
**Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP)** is caused by a deficiency in **porphobilinogen deaminase (PBGD)**, leading to accumulation of PBG and ALA.
Symptoms of **AIP** include the '5 Ps': **Painful abdomen**, **Port wine-colored urine**, **Polyneuropathy**, **Psychological disturbances**, and **Precipitated by drugs** (like barbiturates).
**Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (PCT)** is the most common porphyria, caused by deficiency in **uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase**.
**PCT** clinical presentation typically involves **photosensitivity** and **blistering/hyperpigmentation** of sun-exposed skin.
**Heme** consists of a **protoporphyrin IX** ring and a central **ferrous iron (Fe2+)** atom.
**Hemoglobin (Hb)** is a tetramer with **quaternary structure**, whereas **Myoglobin (Mb)** is a monomer with only **tertiary structure**.
**Myoglobin** has a much higher **affinity for oxygen** compared to hemoglobin, functioning as an oxygen storage unit in muscle.
The **oxygen dissociation curve (ODC)** for Myoglobin is **hyperbolic**, indicating lack of cooperativity.
The **ODC** for Hemoglobin is **sigmoidal (S-shaped)**, which is a result of **positive cooperativity** (binding of one O2 increases affinity for the next).
A **Right Shift** in the ODC means **decreased affinity** for oxygen (easier unloading at tissues), caused by increased **pCO2**, **H+ (low pH)**, **2,3-BPG**, and **Temperature**.
The **Bohr Effect** describes how high **CO2** and **H+** stabilize the **T-state (Tense state)** of hemoglobin to promote oxygen release.
**2,3-Bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG)** is produced in the **Rapoport-Luebering shunt** of glycolysis in RBCs.
**2,3-BPG** binds to the central cavity of the Hb tetramer, stabilizing the **Deoxy-Hb (T-state)** and lowering oxygen affinity.
**Fetal Hemoglobin (HbF)** consists of **2 alpha and 2 gamma chains** (α2γ2).
**HbF** has a **higher affinity** for oxygen than adult hemoglobin (HbA) because it has a **lower affinity for 2,3-BPG**.
The **gamma chains** of HbF lack specific positively charged residues found in the beta chain, reducing the binding pocket's attraction to the negatively charged **2,3-BPG**.
**Carbon Monoxide (CO)** binds to hemoglobin with an affinity **200-250 times higher** than oxygen.
**CO toxicity** causes a **Left Shift** of the ODC, meaning the remaining oxygen bound to Hb is held so tightly it cannot be released to tissues.
Symptoms of **CO poisoning** include **cherry-red skin**, headache, and confusion; treated with **100% O2** or **hyperbaric oxygen**.
**HbA1c** is a glycosylated form of hemoglobin used to monitor long-term **glycemic control** (over 120 days).
**Methemoglobin** contains iron in the **ferric state (Fe3+)**, which cannot bind oxygen.
The **R-state (Relaxed)** of hemoglobin has a high affinity for oxygen and is favored in the **lungs**.
**Zinc protoporphyrin** levels rise in **iron deficiency anemia** and **lead poisoning** as zinc replaces iron in the heme ring.
Heme synthesis occurs partially in the **mitochondria** (1st and last three steps) and partially in the **cytosol**.
**Ferrochelatase** is the final enzyme that inserts **Fe2+** into protoporphyrin IX to form Heme.
**Barbiturates** induce **Cytochrome P450** synthesis, which consumes heme and triggers increased ALA synthase activity, worsening **porphyria attacks**.
**Hematin** is a treatment for porphyria as it provides **negative feedback** to inhibit ALA synthase.
**Sickle Cell Anemia (HbS)** is caused by a point mutation in the **beta-globin gene** where **glutamic acid** is replaced by **valine** at position 6.
The substitution of a polar residue (Glu) for a non-polar one (Val) creates a **hydrophobic patch** on the surface of deoxy-HbS.
**Polymerization** of HbS occurs specifically in the **deoxygenated (T) state**, leading to the characteristic sickle shape.
**Vaso-occlusive crises** in Sickle Cell result from rigid sickle cells blocking microvasculature, leading to tissue **infarction**.
**Hydroxyurea** is a treatment for Sickle Cell that works by **increasing Fetal Hemoglobin (HbF)** levels, which interferes with HbS polymerization.
**Sickle Cell Trait** (heterozygous HbAS) provides a survival advantage against **Plasmodium falciparum malaria**.
**Thalassemia** is a quantitative defect resulting in the reduced synthesis of either **alpha** or **beta** globin chains.
**Beta-Thalassemia Major** (Cooley's Anemia) presents with severe anemia starting at 6 months of life when **HbF** transitions to **HbA**.
Radiological findings in Beta-Thalassemia include **'Crew-cut' appearance** on skull X-ray and **chipmunk facies** due to massive **extramedullary hematopoiesis**.
**Alpha-Thalassemia** is usually caused by **gene deletions** on chromosome 16 (4 total alpha genes).
Deletion of 3 alpha genes results in **HbH disease**, where excess beta chains form tetramers (β4).
Deletion of all 4 alpha genes results in **Hb Barts (γ4)**, causing **Hydrops Fetalis** and intrauterine death because Hb Barts has near-infinite oxygen affinity.
**Methemoglobinemia** presents clinically with **cyanosis** that does not improve with oxygen and **chocolate-colored blood**.
Acquired methemoglobinemia can be caused by exposure to **nitrites**, **sulfonamides**, or local anesthetics like **benzocaine**.
The treatment for acute methemoglobinemia is **Methylene Blue**, which helps reduce Fe3+ back to Fe2+.
**Hypochromic microcytic anemia** is characterized by an **MCV < 80 fL** and low **MCHC**; common causes include **Iron deficiency** and **Thalassemia**.
**Macrocytic anemia** is defined by an **MCV > 100 fL** and is often **megaloblastic**, caused by **Vitamin B12** or **Folate** deficiency.
**Normochromic normocytic anemia** (MCV 80-100 fL) is seen in **acute blood loss**, **hemolysis**, or **anemia of chronic disease**.
In **megaloblastic anemia**, blood smears show **hypersegmented neutrophils** (>5 lobes).
**Sideroblastic anemia** is a microcytic anemia where iron is available but cannot be incorporated into heme; diagnosed by **ringed sideroblasts** in bone marrow.
**Hemoglobin C (HbC)** involves a mutation at the 6th position of the beta chain where **glutamic acid** is replaced by **lysine**.
Patients with **HbSC disease** (one S gene, one C gene) often have clinical severity intermediate between Sickle Cell Trait and Disease.
**Hereditary Spherocytosis** is a defect in RBC membrane proteins like **ankyrin** or **spectrin**, leading to osmotic fragility.
The **Heinz bodies** seen in **G6PD deficiency** are precipitates of denatured hemoglobin.
**Bite cells** (degmacytes) are formed when splenic macrophages pluck out Heinz bodies from RBCs.
**Target cells** (codocytes) are commonly seen on smears of patients with **Thalassemia** and **HbC disease**.
**Iron Deficiency Anemia** unique physical signs include **koilonychia** (spoon nails) and **pica**.
**Pernicious anemia** is a specific type of B12 deficiency caused by lack of **Intrinsic Factor** due to autoimmune destruction of **parietal cells**.
**Reticulocyte count** is used to differentiate between anemia caused by bone marrow failure (low count) and hemolysis (high count).
**Anemia of Chronic Disease** is mediated by **Hepcidin**, which sequesters iron in macrophages by degrading **ferroportin**.
Dietary iron is found in two forms: **Heme iron** (meat) which is highly absorbable, and **Non-heme iron** (plants) which is less absorbable.
Non-heme iron must be in the **ferrous state (Fe2+)** to be absorbed via the **Divalent Metal Transporter 1 (DMT1)**.
**Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)** enhances non-heme iron absorption by reducing **Fe3+ (ferric)** to **Fe2+ (ferrous)**.
**Tannins (tea)**, **oxalates**, and **phytates** inhibit iron absorption by forming insoluble complexes.
**Ferroportin** is the only known iron exporter that allows iron to exit the enterocyte into the blood.
**Hepcidin**, a peptide hormone from the liver, is the master regulator of iron; it binds to and triggers the degradation of **ferroportin**.
In the blood, iron is transported bound to **Transferrin** in the **Fe3+ state** to prevent oxidative damage.
**Total Iron Binding Capacity (TIBC)** is an indirect measure of transferrin levels; it **increases** in iron deficiency.
**Ferritin** is the primary intracellular storage protein for iron; **Serum Ferritin** is the most sensitive marker for total body iron stores.
**Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA)** lab findings: **Low Ferritin**, **High TIBC**, **Low Serum Iron**, and **Low Transferrin Saturation**.
Microscopic findings in IDA include **microcytic, hypochromic RBCs** with increased **central pallor** and **anisocytosis** (variable sizes).
**Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)** requires **Intrinsic Factor (IF)** from gastric parietal cells for absorption in the **terminal ileum**.
**B12 deficiency** leads to accumulation of **Methylmalonic Acid (MMA)**, which causes neurological damage (Subacute Combined Degeneration).
**Folate deficiency** also causes megaloblastic anemia but, unlike B12 deficiency, it does **not** cause elevated MMA or neurological symptoms.
The **'Folate Trap'** refers to the accumulation of 5-methyl-THF in B12 deficiency, which prevents DNA synthesis.
**Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)** deficiency leads to **Sideroblastic Anemia** because it is a cofactor for **ALA synthase**.
**Vitamin C** deficiency (Scurvy) can contribute to anemia due to its role in iron absorption and **folate metabolism**.
**Vitamin K** is essential for the **gamma-carboxylation** of clotting factors **II, VII, IX, and X**, and its deficiency causes bleeding diathesis rather than microcytic anemia.
**Transferrin Saturation** (Serum Iron / TIBC) below **15-20%** is highly suggestive of iron deficiency.
**Soluble Transferrin Receptor (sTfR)** levels increase in iron deficiency but remain normal in anemia of chronic disease.
**Hemosiderin** is an insoluble iron-storage complex seen in cases of iron overload (hemosiderosis).
**Iron absorption** occurs primarily in the **duodenum** and upper jejunum.
**Achlorhydria** (lack of stomach acid) decreases iron absorption because acid helps solubilize iron.
**Erythropoietin (EPO)** stimulates the production of RBCs and decreases **hepcidin** expression to increase iron availability.
**Macrocytic anemia** with **glossitis** (smooth red tongue) suggests B12 or folate deficiency.
**Schilling test** (historically) was used to determine the cause of Vitamin B12 malabsorption.
Oral **ferrous sulfate** is the standard treatment for IDA, often requiring 3-6 months to replenish stores.
High levels of **homocysteine** are found in both B12 and Folate deficiencies.
**Microcytic anemia** with high iron stores and high ferritin suggests **Sideroblastic anemia** or **Lead poisoning**.
The **RDI (Recommended Daily Intake)** for iron is significantly higher in **menstruating women** and **pregnant women**.
Heme degradation occurs primarily in the **macrophages** of the **Reticuloendothelial System (RES)** (Spleen, Liver, Bone Marrow).
The first step is catalyzed by **Heme Oxygenase**, which breaks the porphyrin ring to produce **Biliverdin**, **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, and **Ferrous Iron (Fe2+)**.
**Heme Oxygenase** is the only endogenous source of **Carbon Monoxide** in the human body.
**Biliverdin** (green color) is then reduced to **Bilirubin** (yellow color) by the enzyme **Biliverdin Reductase**.
**Unconjugated Bilirubin (UCB)** is highly hydrophobic and must be transported in the blood bound to **Albumin**.
UCB cannot be excreted in urine because it is **bound to albumin** and not water-soluble.
In the liver, UCB is taken up by hepatocytes and conjugated with **Glucuronic acid** by the enzyme **UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UGT1A1)**.
**Conjugated Bilirubin (CB)** is water-soluble and is actively secreted into the **bile canaliculi**, which is the **rate-limiting step** of hepatic bilirubin metabolism.
In the gut, bacteria deconjugate and reduce bilirubin to form **Urobilinogen**.
Most **urobilinogen** is oxidized by gut bacteria into **Stercobilin**, which gives feces its brown color.
A small portion (approx. 10%) of urobilinogen is reabsorbed and excreted by the kidneys as **Urobilin**, which gives urine its yellow color.
**Enterohepatic circulation** refers to the reabsorption of urobilinogen from the gut back to the liver.
If bile flow is obstructed (**post-hepatic jaundice**), bilirubin does not reach the gut, leading to **pale/clay-colored stools**.
**Direct Bilirubin** is a lab term that roughly corresponds to **Conjugated Bilirubin**.
**Indirect Bilirubin** is calculated (Total - Direct) and corresponds to **Unconjugated Bilirubin**.
**Kernicterus** is a condition where high levels of **UCB** cross the blood-brain barrier and cause neurotoxicity in neonates.
**Van den Bergh reaction** is the chemical assay used to measure bilirubin; direct reacts immediately, indirect requires alcohol.
The enzyme **Heme Oxygenase** requires **NADPH** and **Molecular Oxygen**.
**Albumin** has a high-affinity binding site for bilirubin; drugs like **sulfonamides** can displace bilirubin, increasing risk of kernicterus.
**Ligandin** (GSH S-transferase) binds bilirubin within the hepatocyte to prevent its efflux back into the blood.
**Jaundice (Icterus)** becomes clinically visible when total serum bilirubin exceeds **2-3 mg/dL**.
**Pre-hepatic jaundice** is caused by excessive **hemolysis**, resulting in high levels of **UCB**.
In **Pre-hepatic jaundice**, there is **no bilirubin in urine** (acholuric jaundice) but **increased urinary urobilinogen**.
**Intra-hepatic jaundice** involves damage to hepatocytes (hepatitis), resulting in an increase in both **UCB and CB**.
**Post-hepatic (Obstructive) jaundice** leads to high **CB** and **bilirubinuria** (dark 'tea-colored' urine).
In **complete biliary obstruction**, there is **zero urobilinogen** found in the urine.
**Pruritus** (itching) in obstructive jaundice is often due to the deposition of **bile salts** in the skin.
**Delta bilirubin** is conjugated bilirubin covalently bound to albumin, which has a longer half-life and explains why jaundice lingers after recovery.
**Neonatal Jaundice** is common because newborn **UGT** activity is low and RBC turnover is high.
**Phototherapy** for neonatal jaundice converts UCB into water-soluble **lumirubin** isomers that can be excreted without conjugation.
**Gilbert Syndrome** is a common, mild genetic defect in the **UGT1A1 promoter**, resulting in reduced enzyme activity (about 30%).
In **Gilbert Syndrome**, unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is triggered by **stress**, **fasting**, or **illness**.
**Crigler-Najjar Syndrome Type I** is a total absence of **UGT1A1**, leading to severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia and death from **kernicterus** if untreated.
