Platelet **Alpha-granules** also contain cell adhesion molecules like **fibrin**, **fibronectin**, and **vitronectin** which provide a scaffold for cell migration.
For chronic joint pain, PRP is superior to **Hyaluronic Acid** injections in providing long-term (6-12 months) symptomatic relief.
**Glutathione (GSH)** is a tripeptide composed of three amino acids: **L-Glutamate**, **L-Cysteine**, and **Glycine**.
GSH is often referred to as the **'Master Antioxidant'** due to its high concentration in cells and its ability to regenerate other antioxidants like **Vitamin C** and **Vitamin E**.
The primary mechanism of glutathione in skin whitening is the inhibition of the enzyme **tyrosinase**.
Glutathione inhibits **tyrosinase** directly by chelating the **copper** atoms required for the enzyme's catalytic activity.
A key effect of glutathione is the stimulation of the **pheomelanin** pathway (yellow-red pigment) instead of the **eumelanin** pathway (dark brown-black pigment).
Glutathione quenches **free radicals** and **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)** that otherwise trigger melanogenesis following UV exposure.
The conversion of **L-DOPA** to melanin is intercepted by glutathione, shifting synthesis toward lighter pigments.
Glutathione exists in two states: **reduced (GSH)** and **oxidized (GSSG)**; the ratio of GSH to GSSG is a marker of cellular **oxidative stress**.
The skin-whitening effect is attributed specifically to the **reduced form (GSH)** of glutathione.
**Glutathione Peroxidase** is the enzyme that utilizes GSH to neutralize hydrogen peroxide into water.
**Glutathione Reductase** is the enzyme responsible for converting GSSG back into the active GSH form using **NADPH**.
Oral glutathione has historically been questioned for efficacy due to its degradation by **gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase** in the gastrointestinal tract.
**Intravenous (IV)** administration of glutathione bypasses the GI tract, providing **100% bioavailability**, but carries higher risks of systemic toxicity.
High-dose IV glutathione has been associated with severe adverse effects like **Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS)** and **Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN)**.
Glutathione is used clinically to treat **melasma**, **post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation**, and **solar lentigines**.
The rate-limiting amino acid for the endogenous synthesis of glutathione is **Cysteine**.
Glutathione depletion occurs naturally with **aging**, leading to increased oxidative damage and darker pigmentation in the skin.
Glutathione also inactivates the enzyme **dopachrome tautomerase**, further inhibiting the production of eumelanin.
**Topical glutathione** is often formulated as **oxidized glutathione (GSSG)** in creams, as it is more stable than the reduced form.
Studies suggest that oral doses of **20-40 mg/kg** of glutathione are used for skin lightening, though results vary significantly between individuals.
Glutathione protects the skin from **photoaging** by preventing UV-induced DNA damage in **keratinocytes**.
Common minor side effects of oral glutathione include **abdominal cramps**, **bloating**, and **allergic rashes**.
Chronic high-dose use of glutathione may potentially interfere with **thyroid function** and **renal clearance**.
The 'whitening' effect of glutathione is not permanent and requires maintenance as **melanocytes** continue to produce pigment.
Glutathione acts as a **detoxifying agent** in the liver by conjugating with toxins via the enzyme **Glutathione S-transferase (GST)**.
In the **Gamma-glutamyl cycle**, glutathione helps transport amino acids across the cell membrane.
Combining glutathione with **Vitamin C** (Ascorbic Acid) is common because Vitamin C helps keep glutathione in its **reduced state**.
The use of glutathione for 'skin whitening' is considered an **off-label** use by many regulatory bodies like the **FDA**.
Glutathione is highly concentrated in the **liver**, **kidneys**, and the **lens of the eye**.
**N-acetylcysteine (NAC)** is often administered as a precursor to boost endogenous glutathione levels.
Systemic glutathione administration can cause a uniform lightening of the skin tone, unlike topical agents which are **localized**.
The inhibition of **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)** by glutathione prevents the activation of **NF-κB**, which otherwise increases melanin production.
Glutathione levels are notably lower in individuals with **Type 2 Diabetes** and other age-related metabolic disorders.
In melanocytes, glutathione binds to the active site of tyrosinase, preventing the binding of **Tyrosine**.
Excessive glutathione might theoretically cause **hypopigmented patches** or vitiligo-like lesions if not monitored.
Glutathione synthesis is an **ATP-dependent** two-step process involving **glutamate-cysteine ligase** and **glutathione synthetase**.
The antioxidant role of glutathione is essential for protecting the **mitochondria** from oxidative decay during the aging process.
Glutathione esters and **liposomal glutathione** are modern formulations designed to improve oral absorption and bypass degradation.
While glutathione is marketed for whitening, its primary biological role is maintaining cellular **redox homeostasis**.
Patients on long-term glutathione should have periodic **liver function tests (LFTs)** and **renal function tests (RFTs)** to ensure safety.
